</step>

The third rule links wisdom to logical reasoning, giving us a way to derive Olga's logical status from her known wisdom. This will be relevant when we consider the disjunction involving Olga's logic and consistency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Wisdom gives rise to logical reasoning; therefore, Olga's wisdom entails that she is logical.</text>
<logic>~impartial(jules) :- ambitious(jules)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that discipline leads to proficiency. This creates a bridge from a potential property of Jules (discipline) to the very attribute we are questioning—proficiency.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Discipline leads to proficiency, meaning that if Jules is disciplined, he becomes proficient.</text>
<logic>ambitious(jules) v disciplined(jules)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says ambition causes a lack of impartiality. This contrapositive relationship will later allow us to infer the absence of ambition from the presence of impartiality.

<logic>fit(jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Jules is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Jules is not ambitious.</text>
<logic>~ambitious(jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Jules is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(jules)</logic>
</step>

By combining the fact that Jules is fit with the rule that a fit person is impartial, we can directly infer Jules' impartiality. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens: the antecedent (fit) is true, so the consequent (impartial) follows.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Jules being fit and the rule that fit implies impartial, we infer that Jules is impartial.</text>
<logic>proficient(jules)</logic>
</step>

We derived Jules is proficient, so the conclusion Jules is not proficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Olive is a specialty ingredient that nutritionists often examine for its health benefits. They note that olive is low‑sugar, which can affect other nutritional properties. examples.question: Based on the given facts and rules, can we conclude that olive is not high‑protein?

The second rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if olive were high‑protein, it could not be an antioxidant. This creates a tension that will let us eliminate the high‑protein possibility once we know olive is an antioxidant.

The disjunction states that either olive lacks fiber‑richness or it possesses vitamin‑C enrichment. When we later learn that olive is fiber‑rich, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that olive must be vitamin‑C enriched.

The fact that olive is low‑sugar gives us a concrete starting point. It will interact with the fourth disjunction to produce a new property, namely fiber‑richness.

Applying disjunctive syllogism to the low‑sugar fact and the fourth disjunction, we eliminate the "not low‑sugar" option, leaving the conclusion that olive is fiber‑rich. This step converts the abstract choice into a definite attribute.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">

<logic>fiber_rich(olive)</logic>
</step>

Now we take the newly obtained fiber‑rich property and use it in the first disjunction. Because the "not fiber‑rich" part is false, the disjunction can only be satisfied by olive being vitamin‑C enriched, so we derive that property.

We now have olive as an antioxidant, while the second rule says a high‑protein olive would be not antioxidant. By modus tollens, the presence of the antioxidant forces the negation of high‑protein, so we conclude olive is not high‑protein.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because olive is an antioxidant and high‑protein would make it not an antioxidant, we infer that olive cannot be high‑protein.</text>

Some designers claim that a model who embraces experimental concepts will not be courteous, while a model who is well_fitted is said to hinder efficiency. Finally, the fashion press constantly debates whether the model is either experimental or trend_aware.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that the runway_model is well_fitted?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the runway_model possesses the property well_fitted. To answer this, we will trace the logical connections from the known fact that the model is vibrant through the chain of rules provided, ultimately checking if a well_fitted status can be sustained.

This rule tells us that any model labeled stylish automatically gains the sporty attribute. It sets up a forward implication that will become useful once we can establish stylishness later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the model is stylish, then the model is sporty.</text>
<logic>sporty(runway_model) :- stylish(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that trend_aware status guarantees stylishness. This provides a bridge from the more observable trend awareness to the more abstract stylish quality, which will later trigger the sporty rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the model is trend_aware, then the model is stylish.</text>
<logic>stylish(runway_model) :- trend_aware(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links vibrancy to courtesy, indicating that a vibrant model will behave courteously. Since we already have a fact about vibrancy, this rule will let us derive courtesy soon.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the model is vibrant, then the model is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(runway_model) :- vibrant(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, any sporty model is also efficient. This will allow us to move from sporty (once derived) to efficiency, which will later be used to argue about well_fittedness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the model is sporty, then the model is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(runway_model) :- sporty(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

This rule states that experimental models cannot be courteous. It introduces a negative relationship that will help us eliminate the experimental possibility once we know the model is courteous.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the model is experimental, then the model is not courteous.</text>
<logic>~courteous(runway_model) :- experimental(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule says that if a model were well_fitted, it could not be efficient. This contrapositive relationship will be crucial for showing that well_fittedness leads to a contradiction with the efficiency we will later obtain.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the model is well_fitted, then the model is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(runway_model) :- well_fitted(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction informs us that the model must be either experimental or trend_aware. This logical alternative will be resolved once we have information about courtesy, allowing us to pick the correct side of the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either the model is experimental or the model is trend_aware.</text>
<logic>experimental(runway_model) v trend_aware(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

The fact directly asserts that the runway_model is vibrant. This is our starting point for the forward chain of reasoning, as vibrancy triggers courtesy via PREMISE_3.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The runway_model is vibrant.</text>
<logic>vibrant(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the model is vibrant together with PREMISE_3, we infer courtesy. The rule explicitly says vibrant implies courteous, so we can safely add courteous to our knowledge base.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>From the model being vibrant we infer that the model is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have courtesy, PREMISE_5 tells us that an experimental model would be not courteous. Since we have courtesy, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that the model is not experimental. This is a classic modus tollens step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Because the model is courteous, we can infer that the model is not experimental.</text>
<logic>~experimental(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

We have established that the model is not experimental, and PREMISE_7 says the model is either experimental or trend_aware. By eliminating the experimental alternative, the disjunction forces the trend_aware option to be true. This is a direct application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Since the model is not experimental and either experimental or trend_aware holds, the model must be trend_aware.</text>
<logic>trend_aware(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

With trend_aware established, PREMISE_2 allows us to infer stylishness because trend_aware guarantees stylish. This moves us one step closer to the sporty property needed for later deductions.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Being trend_aware allows us to conclude that the model is stylish.</text>
<logic>stylish(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

Having derived stylishness, we can now apply PREMISE_1, which converts stylish into sporty. This chain from trend_aware to stylish to sporty is now complete.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>From being stylish we deduce that the model is sporty.</text>
<logic>sporty(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

Since the model is sporty, PREMISE_4 tells us that sporty implies efficiency. Therefore, we can add efficient to our list of derived properties.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Given the model is sporty, we infer that the model is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

We now possess efficiency, and PREMISE_6 states that a well_fitted model would not be efficient. By contraposition (modus tollens), the presence of efficiency forces the negation of well_fitted, giving us ~well_fitted.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>Because the model is efficient, we can conclude that the model is not well_fitted.</text>
<logic>~well_fitted(runway_model)</logic>
</step>

We derived Runway_model is not well fitted, so the conclusion Runway_model is well fitted is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the pantheon of Greek myth, Hermes is renowned as the swift messenger of the gods. Legends describe him as ancient, having witnessed countless mortal affairs, and this long experience grants him a heroic bearing. Those who are heroic are said to possess a prophetic mind, allowing them to foresee the outcomes of their swift journeys. Prophetic Hermes, in turn, is considered omniscient, knowing the secrets of both Olympus and the earth. Finally, the myths hold that a being who is not omniscient must at least be a sage, a figure of deep wisdom, creating a subtle logical tension in the stories.
examples.question: Given the information about Hermes, can we conclude that Hermes is not a sage?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether the statement that Hermes is not a sage holds given a set of mythological facts and logical rules. The goal is to see if the knowledge about Hermes inevitably leads to him being a sage, thereby falsifying the negation.

The first premise connects Hermes's antiquity to a heroic nature; this link will let us move from the known fact that he is ancient to a new property we can later use. It is essential because heroic status triggers further inferences in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Hermes is ancient, then he is heroic.</text>
<logic>heroic(hermes) :- ancient(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that any prophetic Hermes must also be omniscient. This will become relevant after we establish that Hermes is prophetic, allowing us to reach the omniscient attribute needed for the disjunction analysis.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Hermes is prophetic, then he is omniscient.</text>
<logic>omniscient(hermes) :- prophetic(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides a bridge from heroic to prophetic, meaning that once we have heroic Hermes we can immediately claim he possesses prophetic insight. This step is crucial for progressing toward omniscience.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Hermes is heroic, then he is prophetic.</text>
<logic>prophetic(hermes) :- heroic(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers a logical choice: either Hermes lacks omniscience or he is a sage. When we later demonstrate that Hermes is indeed omniscient, the first part of this disjunction becomes false, forcing the second part—being a sage—to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Hermes is not omniscient or he is a sage.</text>
<logic>~omniscient(hermes) v sage(hermes)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise directly states that Hermes is ancient, giving us a solid starting point for the deductive chain. This fact will activate the first rule and set the entire inference in motion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Hermes is ancient.</text>
<logic>ancient(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact that Hermes is ancient with the rule that ancient implies heroic, concluding that Hermes is heroic. This deduction is straightforward: the antecedent of the rule is satisfied, so the consequent must hold.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Hermes being ancient and the first rule, we infer Hermes is heroic.</text>
<logic>heroic(hermes)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Hermes is heroic, we can use the third rule, again via modus ponens, to infer that Hermes is prophetic. The heroic condition matches the rule's premise, allowing us to draw the prophetic conclusion confidently.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given Hermes is heroic and the third rule, we derive that Hermes is prophetic.</text>
<logic>prophetic(hermes)</logic>
</step>

With Hermes established as prophetic, the second rule lets us infer omniscience through another application of modus ponens. The prophetic antecedent is met, so the consequent—being omniscient—follows directly.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since Hermes is prophetic and the second rule applies, we conclude Hermes is omniscient.</text>
<logic>omniscient(hermes)</logic>
</step>

We now have Hermes as omniscient, which makes the left side of the disjunction (~omniscient) false. By disjunctive syllogism, the only way the disjunction can remain true is if the right side, Hermes being a sage, is true. Hence we deduce that Hermes is a sage.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>With Hermes being omniscient and the disjunction, we resolve that Hermes must be a sage.</text>
<logic>sage(hermes)</logic>
</step>

We derived Hermes is sage, so the conclusion Hermes is not sage is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In a clinical trial, a particular patient is being observed for both psychological and therapeutic traits. The medical team notes that the patient sometimes displays innovative problem‑solving during therapy sessions, while at other times the patient appears very calm. Additionally, the staff has recorded that the patient follows the prescribed regimen faithfully. The researchers are interested in whether the patient’s overall demeanor can be described as courteous toward the healthcare providers.
examples.question: Does the patient exhibit courteous behavior?
examples.completion:
<think>
The problem asks us to determine whether the patient can be described as courteous. To answer this, we must trace a chain of logical connections from the facts we have to the courteous property.

The first rule links the patient’s innovative attitude to being responsive, which will later help us reach courteousness. Recognizing this connection is essential because it provides a way to move from an abstract quality (innovation) to a more concrete therapeutic trait (responsiveness).
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the patient shows innovative thinking, they are considered responsive to treatment.</text>
<logic>responsive(patient) :- innovative(patient)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that responsiveness directly yields courteous behavior. This bridge is crucial because it connects the intermediate trait we will derive (responsiveness) to the final trait we need to prove (courteous).
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the patient is responsive, then they behave courteously.</text>
<logic>courteous(patient) :- responsive(patient)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that if the patient is calm, then they would not be compliant. This relationship will allow us to eliminate the possibility of calmness later, using the known fact about compliance.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being calm leads to the patient not being compliant.</text>
<logic>~compliant(patient) :- calm(patient)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two mutually exclusive possibilities for the patient: either they are calm or they are innovative. This will become a decisive point once we rule out one of the options.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the patient is calm or the patient is innovative.</text>
<logic>calm(patient) v innovative(patient)</logic>
</step>

We are given explicitly that the patient is compliant, which serves as a solid starting fact. This fact will clash with the implication from calmness, enabling us to deduce that the patient cannot be calm.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is known that the patient is compliant.</text>
<logic>compliant(patient)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the patient is compliant, and the rule says calmness would make the patient non‑compliant, we infer that calmness must be false for this patient. This reasoning eliminates the calm alternative from the disjunction, setting the stage for the next inference.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since the patient is compliant and calm would imply non‑compliance, the patient cannot be calm.</text>
<logic>~calm(patient)</logic>
</step>

With the calm option ruled out and the disjunction stating the patient is either calm or innovative, the only remaining possibility is that the patient is innovative. This step converts the abstract disjunction into a concrete attribute we can work with.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Given that the patient is not calm and that the patient must be either calm or innovative, we infer that the patient is innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(patient)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the patient is innovative, we can apply the first rule which says innovation leads to responsiveness. This yields the responsive trait, moving us one step closer to courteousness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From the patient being innovative and the rule that innovation implies responsiveness, we conclude the patient is responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(patient)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that the patient is responsive, we invoke the second rule that responsive patients behave courteously. This directly gives us the courteous property we set out to prove.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because the patient is responsive and responsiveness entails courteous behavior, the patient is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(patient)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Patient is courteous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The first rule tells us that eagerness leads to strength for Lara. This connection is crucial because it links the known fact about her enthusiasm to the intermediate property of being strong, which will later help us reach calmness.

<text>If Lara is eager, then she is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(climber_lara) :- eager(climber_lara)</logic>

The second rule establishes that strength results in calmness for Lara. It serves as the final bridge in the logical chain, allowing us to move from the intermediate state of strength to the target state of calmness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Lara is strong, then she is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(climber_lara) :- strong(climber_lara)</logic>

We are explicitly told that Lara is eager, which gives us a concrete starting point. This fact activates the first rule, enabling us to move forward in the reasoning process.

<logic>eager(climber_lara)</logic>
</step>

By combining Lara's eagerness with the rule that eagerness implies strength, we can directly infer that Lara is strong. This step follows the pattern of modus ponens: a known antecedent and a conditional statement together yield the consequent.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because Lara is eager and the first rule holds, we can infer that Lara is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(climber_lara)</logic>

Having established that Lara is strong, we now apply the second rule, which says that strength leads to calmness. Using modus ponens again, the presence of the antecedent (strength) allows us to deduce the consequent, namely that Lara is calm.

Therefore Climber_lara is calm, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Deep within the mountain range lies a prominent granite rock that geologists have been studying. The rock's surface is clearly exposed, making its characteristics easy to observe. Various logical relationships have been noted: whenever a rock is exposed it tends to be hard, a crystalline structure often leads to a porous texture, and a weathered rock cannot be porous. Moreover, either the rock is not hard or it exhibits a crystalline pattern. These observations set the stage for a careful deduction about the rock's weathering state.
examples.question: Based on the given facts and rules, can we conclude that the granite rock is not weathered?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of geological facts and conditional statements about a particular granite rock. Our goal is to determine whether the rock can be shown to be not weathered, i.e., to establish the negation of the weathered property.

The rule linking exposure to hardness tells us that any rock whose surface is visible will also be hard. This connection will be useful because we already know the rock is exposed, allowing us to derive hardness later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the granite rock is exposed, then it is hard.</text>
<logic>hard(granite_rock) :- exposed(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that a crystalline structure guarantees porosity. Since we will later infer that the rock is crystalline, this rule will let us move from crystallinity to porosity, a key step for the final contradiction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the granite rock is crystalline, then it is porous.</text>
<logic>porous(granite_rock) :- crystalline(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

The third rule gives a contrapositive relationship: a weathered rock cannot be porous. By establishing porosity, we will be able to infer that the rock cannot be weathered, which is exactly what we need to prove.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the granite rock is weathered, then it is not porous.</text>
<logic>~porous(granite_rock) :- weathered(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction expresses that at least one of two conditions holds: either the rock is not hard, or it is crystalline. This logical alternative will allow us to eliminate the "not hard" option once we know the rock is hard, forcing the crystalline alternative.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the granite rock is not hard, or it is crystalline.</text>
<logic>~hard(granite_rock) v crystalline(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the granite rock is exposed is our starting point. It directly activates the first rule, enabling us to infer hardness in the next step.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The granite rock is exposed.</text>
<logic>exposed(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the rock is exposed together with the rule that exposure implies hardness, we can apply modus ponens to conclude the rock is hard. This derived hardness will later allow us to resolve the disjunction by discarding the "not hard" possibility.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Therefore, the granite rock is hard.</text>
<logic>hard(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the rock is hard, the disjunction "not hard or crystalline" cannot be satisfied by the first disjunct, so the second disjunct must be true. Hence, we infer that the rock is crystalline, employing disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Thus, the granite rock is crystalline.</text>
<logic>crystalline(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

Having established crystallinity, we can invoke the second rule, which says a crystalline rock is porous. Applying modus ponens again yields that the granite rock is porous.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Consequently, the granite rock is porous.</text>
<logic>porous(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

We now have porosity, but the third rule tells us that a weathered rock would be not porous. By modus tollens, if the rock were weathered it would contradict its porosity, so we must conclude the rock is not weathered.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Hence, the granite rock is not weathered.</text>
<logic>~weathered(granite_rock)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Granite_rock is not weathered, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

With Master Lee confirmed as fit, we turn to PREMISE_2, which states either not fit or steady. The not‑fit clause is now false, so the steady alternative must be true. Consequently, we deduce that Master Lee is steady.

The fact that RoboFelix is on_time directly triggers the rule that on_time robots behave courteously. By applying this conditional, we can safely assert that RoboFelix is courteous, establishing the first link in the chain toward durability.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From being on_time we infer that RoboFelix is courteous.</text>

Having established courtesy, we use the rule that courteous robots are durable. This step is straightforward: the antecedent (courteous) is satisfied, so the consequent (durable) follows, giving us a sturdier robot to work with.

Durability then activates the rule that durable robots remain calibrated. Since we already know RoboFelix is durable, we can conclude it maintains its calibration, which will later be important for evaluating speed and brightness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">

<logic>calibrated(robo_felix)</logic>

We now have a calibrated robot, but there is a rule stating that if a robot were sensor_aware, it would not be calibrated. Because RoboFelix is calibrated, the sensor_aware condition must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence we infer that RoboFelix is not sensor_aware.

<text>Knowing that RoboFelix is calibrated and that sensor_aware would make it uncalibrated, we infer that RoboFelix is not sensor_aware.</text>

The disjunction tells us RoboFelix is either sensor_aware or algorithmic. Since we just ruled out sensor_aware, the only remaining possibility is that RoboFelix is algorithmic. This reasoning eliminates one option and forces the other to hold.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">

</step>

With RoboFelix being algorithmic, we can apply the rule that algorithmic robots are motivated. The antecedent is satisfied, so the consequent (motivated) is derived, moving us closer to high speed.

Finally, the rule linking high_speed to brightness tells us that any high_speed robot is bright. Since RoboFelix is high_speed, we conclude that it is indeed bright, establishing the positive attribute we were investigating.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">

We derived Robo_felix is bright, so the conclusion Robo_felix is not bright is False.

examples.context: Yuri is a seasoned mission specialist aboard the orbital telescope Aurora, cruising through the Kuiper Belt. The crew notes that he always checks his equipment before a launch, showing a high degree of preparation. Because of this meticulous habit, his teammates consider him reliable in every critical operation. In the quiet of deep space, a reliable astronaut tends to stay focused and attentive to the instruments, which can sometimes make him feel uneasy about the unknowns surrounding the mission.
examples.question: Given the information about Yuri's preparation and the logical connections between preparation, reliability, attentiveness, and anxiety, can we conclude that Yuri is anxious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Yuri feels anxious based on a chain of logical conditions. The goal is to start from the concrete fact that Yuri is prepared and follow the given rules until we can reach a statement about his anxiety.

The first rule links attentiveness directly to anxiety, so once we know Yuri is attentive we will be able to assert his anxiety. This premise will be the final link in our reasoning chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Yuri is attentive, then he becomes anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(yuri) :- attentive(yuri)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that reliability guarantees attentiveness. It provides the bridge between being reliable and the later step where we need attentiveness to infer anxiety.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Yuri is reliable, then he is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(yuri) :- reliable(yuri)</logic>
</step>

The third rule shows that preparation implies reliability. This is crucial because our only starting fact is that Yuri is prepared, and we need to convert that into reliability.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Yuri is prepared, then he is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(yuri) :- prepared(yuri)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Yuri is prepared, which gives us a solid base fact to apply the preparation‑to‑reliability rule. Without this fact, the chain could not get started.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a known fact that Yuri is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(yuri)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens, we combine the fact that Yuri is prepared with the rule that preparation leads to reliability. Because the antecedent (prepared) holds, the consequent (reliable) must also hold, so we conclude Yuri is reliable. This new fact moves us one step closer to attentiveness.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From Yuri being prepared and the rule that preparation leads to reliability, we infer that Yuri is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(yuri)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Yuri's reliability, we use the second rule. The rule states that reliability guarantees attentiveness, and since the antecedent (reliable) is true, we can infer the consequent (attentive). This gives us the condition needed for the final anxiety rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since Yuri is reliable and reliability implies attentiveness, we conclude that Yuri is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(yuri)</logic>
</step>

With Yuri confirmed as attentive, we invoke the first rule which says attentiveness leads to anxiety. Because the antecedent (attentive) is satisfied, the consequent (anxious) follows, allowing us to assert that Yuri is anxious.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Given that Yuri is attentive and attentiveness triggers anxiety, we deduce that Yuri is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(yuri)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Yuri is anxious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Sprinter John has been a key member of the national track team for several seasons. His coaches note that when he concentrates on his start technique, his overall demeanor on the track becomes notably sportsmanlike. The training staff also keep track of two opposing possibilities for each athlete: either the runner is not yet fully conditioned, or the runner already possesses a high level of skill. Likewise, an athlete who fails to show sportsmanship might still be in the process of becoming conditioned. All of these observations help the team predict future performance.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is sprinter John skilled?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether sprinter John possesses the quality of skill. To answer, we will chain together the facts and logical rules provided, moving from what is known about his determination toward the target property of being skilled.

The rule links the property of being determined directly to being sportsmanlike, which will let us convert a concrete fact about John into another useful attribute. This connection is essential because later steps rely on knowing whether John is sportsmanlike.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever John is determined, he is also sportsmanlike.</text>
<logic>sportsmanlike(sprinter_john) :- determined(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: John might lack conditioning, or he might already be skilled. This statement will become pivotal once we establish John's conditioning status, allowing us to eliminate one side and claim the other.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either John is not conditioned or John is skilled.</text>
<logic>~conditioned(sprinter_john) v skilled(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction similarly gives two options: either John is not sportsmanlike, or he is conditioned. Once we can assert that John is indeed sportsmanlike, the disjunction forces the conclusion that he must be conditioned.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either John is not sportsmanlike or John is conditioned.</text>
<logic>~sportsmanlike(sprinter_john) v conditioned(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

The fact that John is determined provides the concrete starting point for the reasoning chain. It directly activates the rule in PREMISE_1, enabling us to derive a new attribute about John.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>John is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

By applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact that John is determined, we infer that John is sportsmanlike. This step translates a mental focus into a behavioral quality, setting up the condition needed for the next disjunctive inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From John's determination and the rule, we conclude that John is sportsmanlike.</text>
<logic>sportsmanlike(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know John is sportsmanlike, the disjunction in PREMISE_3 (either not sportsmanlike or conditioned) cannot be satisfied by the first alternative. Consequently, the only remaining possibility is that John is conditioned. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism: the truth of one disjunct eliminates the other.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since John is sportsmanlike and the disjunction says either not sportsmanlike or conditioned, we infer that John is conditioned.</text>
<logic>conditioned(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

Having established that John is conditioned, we turn to the disjunction in PREMISE_2 (either not conditioned or skilled). Since the first option is false, the second must hold, allowing us to conclude that John is skilled. Again, the disjunctive syllogism lets us drop the negated side and keep the affirmative one.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that John is conditioned and the first disjunction states either not conditioned or skilled, we deduce that John is skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(sprinter_john)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sprinter_john is skilled, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Firm Aspen operates in a competitive market and has earned a reputation for being risk averse. Analysts note that a risk‑averse company often follows an ethical code, and that ethical firms tend to act aggressively in pursuing opportunities. When a firm is aggressive, it usually becomes profit‑seeking, while profit‑seeking firms are known to be efficient. Moreover, firms that investigate market trends become long‑term oriented, and long‑term orientation typically leads to stability. Finally, a firm cannot be both profit‑seeking and socially responsible at the same time, and the market report lists Aspen as either socially responsible or market investigative.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Firm Aspen is efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with a set of traits describing Firm Aspen and a collection of conditional statements linking those traits. Our goal is to determine whether the information ultimately forces Aspen to be efficient.

The first rule tells us that a firm that looks to the long term will exhibit stability in its operations. This connection will be useful later when we manage to prove Aspen is long‑term oriented.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is long‑term oriented, then Aspen is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(firm_aspen) :- long_term_oriented(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links an aggressive stance to profit‑seeking behavior, establishing a path from aggression to a financially motivated attitude. This will allow us to move from ethical to profit‑seeking once we have aggression.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is aggressive, then Aspen is profit‑seeking.</text>
<logic>profit_seeking(firm_aspen) :- aggressive(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Here we see that a firm that actively investigates the market becomes oriented toward long‑term planning. This bridge will let us travel from market investigation to stability via the previous rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is market investigative, then Aspen is long‑term oriented.</text>
<logic>long_term_oriented(firm_aspen) :- market_investigative(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that being risk averse inevitably makes a firm adhere to ethical principles. Since we already know Aspen is risk averse, this rule will let us derive ethical conduct.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is risk averse, then Aspen is ethical.</text>
<logic>ethical(firm_aspen) :- risk_averse(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Rule five connects ethical behavior to an aggressive approach, providing the missing link between risk aversion and aggression. Once we have ethics, we can claim aggression.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is ethical, then Aspen is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(firm_aspen) :- ethical(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule is crucial because it directly ties stability to efficiency, the final property we care about. When we finally establish stability, this rule will give us efficiency immediately.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is stable, then Aspen is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(firm_aspen) :- stable(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

This rule expresses an incompatibility: a socially responsible firm cannot be profit‑seeking. It will allow us to eliminate the socially responsible alternative once we know Aspen is profit‑seeking.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Aspen is socially responsible, then Aspen is not profit‑seeking.</text>
<logic>~profit_seeking(firm_aspen) :- socially_responsible(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Aspen must be either socially responsible or market investigative. Together with the previous rule, this will force us to accept the market investigative option.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Aspen is socially responsible or market investigative.</text>
<logic>socially_responsible(firm_aspen) v market_investigative(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

We are given as a fact that Aspen is risk averse, which is the starting point for the chain of deductions that follow.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Aspen is risk averse.</text>
<logic>risk_averse(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Because we have the fact that Aspen is risk averse and the rule that risk aversion implies ethical behavior, we can directly infer that Aspen is ethical. This is a straightforward application of the conditional.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>From Aspen being risk averse and the rule that risk averse implies ethical, we infer that Aspen is ethical.</text>
<logic>ethical(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Having established Aspen's ethical nature, we invoke the rule that ethical firms act aggressively. By applying the rule, we conclude that Aspen is aggressive.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Using the fact that ethical implies aggressive, we conclude that Aspen is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

With aggression in hand, we turn to the rule linking aggression to profit‑seeking. The rule tells us that an aggressive firm will be profit‑seeking, so we deduce that Aspen is profit‑seeking.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Since aggressive leads to profit‑seeking, Aspen becomes profit‑seeking.</text>
<logic>profit_seeking(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Aspen as profit‑seeking, which clashes with the rule that a socially responsible firm cannot be profit‑seeking. This contradiction forces us to reject the possibility that Aspen is socially responsible, giving us ~responsible(aspen).
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Because profit‑seeking contradicts the rule that socially responsible firms are not profit‑seeking, we deduce that Aspen cannot be socially responsible.</text>
<logic>~socially_responsible(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Given the disjunction that Aspen is either socially responsible or market investigative, and knowing that the socially responsible option is false, the only remaining way to satisfy the disjunction is that Aspen is market investigative. Thus we derive curiosity.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>Given the disjunction that Aspen is socially responsible or market investigative, and knowing Aspen is not socially responsible, we infer that Aspen is market investigative.</text>
<logic>market_investigative(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that Aspen is market investigative, we use the rule stating that such investigation leads to a long‑term orientation. This yields the patient property for Aspen.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>From market investigative we obtain that Aspen is long‑term oriented.</text>
<logic>long_term_oriented(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Now that Aspen is long‑term oriented, the first rule tells us that long‑term orientation guarantees stability. Applying this rule gives us consistency for Aspen.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,1">
<text>Long‑term orientation yields stability for Aspen.</text>
<logic>stable(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with Aspen established as stable, the sixth rule directly provides that a stable firm is efficient. This completes the chain, giving us the resourceful (efficient) property.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,6">
<text>Stability then gives us that Aspen is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(firm_aspen)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Firm_aspen is efficient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the grand theater, the principal dancer of the ballet troupe is celebrated for her flawless technique and unwavering poise. She is known to be deeply concentrated during rehearsals, and the artistic director often remarks on her discipline on stage. The company wonders whether her reputation for experience matches the expectations of the audience.
examples.question: Is it true that the principal dancer is not experienced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the principal dancer lacks experience. The logical puzzle supplies facts about her concentration and two conditional statements that connect discipline and experience, so we must chain these to address the query.

The first disjunction links the dancer's discipline to her experience, giving us two possible ways the statement could be true. Keeping it in mind is crucial because later we will use it together with a derived fact about discipline.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Either the principal dancer is not disciplined, or she is experienced.</text>
<logic>~disciplined(principal_dancer) v experienced(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction ties the dancer's concentration to her discipline, offering another pair of alternatives. This relationship will allow us to replace the unknown discipline status with a known concentration fact.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either the principal dancer is not concentrated, or she is disciplined.</text>
<logic>~concentrated(principal_dancer) v disciplined(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

We are told explicitly that the principal dancer is concentrated during rehearsals. This concrete fact will serve as the anchor point for applying disjunctive syllogism to the second disjunction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The principal dancer is concentrated.</text>
<logic>concentrated(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

Since the dancer is concentrated, the clause "not concentrated" in the second disjunction is false. Disjunctive syllogism then permits us to discard that false part, leaving only the remaining option: the dancer must be disciplined. This gives us a new, reliable fact about her discipline.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because we know she is concentrated and the second disjunction says "not concentrated or disciplined," we can discard the first part and conclude that the principal dancer is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established she is disciplined, we turn to the first disjunction. The term "not disciplined" is false because we just proved she is disciplined, so disjunctive syllogism again forces us to accept the other alternative: the dancer is experienced. Thus we derive her experience from the earlier steps.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Having established that she is disciplined, the first disjunction "not disciplined or experienced" lets us drop the "not disciplined" option, yielding that the principal dancer is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

We derived Principal_dancer is experienced, so the conclusion Principal_dancer is not experienced is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Sutton is a well‑known photographer who prefers a careful, steady‑handed approach when framing a shot. In the studio, photographers who are steady‑handed are often described as dynamic, because they can quickly adjust lighting and composition. Dynamic photographers, in turn, tend to develop a storytelling style that captures the emotions of a scene. However, those who are highly detail‑oriented usually avoid a storytelling focus, as their emphasis on minutiae can clash with narrative flow. Given these conventions, we have a set of observations and rules about Sutton's photographic style.
examples.question: Can we logically conclude that Sutton is not detail‑oriented?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Sutton lacks the detail‑oriented trait based on a collection of photographic style rules. The goal is to chain the given facts and implications to reach a conclusion about Sutton's detail orientation.

The first rule links the dynamic quality of a photographer to a storytelling approach. This connection will be useful later when we know Sutton is dynamic, allowing us to infer storytelling.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever a photographer is dynamic, they tend to adopt a storytelling style.</text>
<logic>storytelling(sutton) :- dynamic(sutton)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being steady‑handed implies being dynamic. Since we have a fact about Sutton's steadiness, this rule will let us move from that fact to a dynamic status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a photographer is steady‑handed, then they are considered dynamic.</text>
<logic>dynamic(sutton) :- steady_handed(sutton)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that any detail‑oriented photographer cannot be storytelling. This contrapositive relationship will be crucial for deriving the negation of detail orientation once we establish Sutton is storytelling.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a photographer is detail‑oriented, they are not storytelling.</text>
<logic>~storytelling(sutton) :- detail_oriented(sutton)</logic>
</step>

The factual premise informs us that Sutton possesses the steady‑handed characteristic. This concrete observation is the starting point for the chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Sutton is known for being steady‑handed.</text>
<logic>steady_handed(sutton)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Sutton is steady‑handed with the rule that steady‑handed photographers are dynamic, we apply Modus Ponens to obtain that Sutton is dynamic. This step translates the general rule into a specific attribute for Sutton.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Sutton is steady‑handed and steady‑handed photographers are dynamic, we infer that Sutton is dynamic.</text>
<logic>dynamic(sutton)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Sutton is dynamic, we can use the first rule, which says dynamic photographers are storytelling. Again by Modus Ponens, we conclude Sutton adopts a storytelling style.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because Sutton is dynamic and dynamic photographers are storytelling, we conclude that Sutton is storytelling.</text>
<logic>storytelling(sutton)</logic>
</step>

We have Sutton being storytelling, and we also have the rule that detail‑oriented photographers are not storytelling. Using Modus Tollens, the presence of storytelling forces the negation of the detail‑oriented condition, so we infer that Sutton is not detail‑oriented.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given Sutton is storytelling and the rule that detail‑oriented photographers are not storytelling, we deduce that Sutton cannot be detail‑oriented.</text>
<logic>~detail_oriented(sutton)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sutton is not detail oriented, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the laboratory, two autonomous units, alpha_bot and beta_bot, are being evaluated for a series of operational traits. The engineers know that if a robot is self‑aware, it will act anticipatorily, and that an adaptive robot is automatically skilled. Whenever a robot runs at high power, it is considered fault‑tolerant, and a calibrated robot cannot be anticipatory. Moreover, the system logs reveal that either alpha_bot is not skilled or it runs at high power, and that either beta_bot is not compliant or it is self‑aware.

We need to determine whether beta_bot fails the calibration check. The target statement is the negation of the calibrated predicate for beta_bot, so we must derive ~calibrated(beta_bot) from the supplied facts and rules.

The first rule links self‑awareness to anticipatory behavior for any robot. This connection will be crucial later because establishing beta_bot's self‑awareness will allow us to infer its anticipatory status, which directly opposes calibration.
<step n="1" type="premise">

The second rule tells us that an adaptive robot automatically becomes skilled. Although it concerns alpha_bot, it will later interact with the disjunction about alpha_bot's skill level, helping us to resolve that clause.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If alpha_bot is adaptive, then it is skilled.</text>
<logic>~anticipatory(beta_bot) :- calibrated(beta_bot)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that high‑power operation yields fault tolerance. This rule is paired with the disjunction concerning alpha_bot, providing a way to satisfy the disjunction if the skilled alternative fails.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If alpha_bot runs at high power, then it becomes fault‑tolerant.</text>
<logic>~compliant(beta_bot) v self_aware(beta_bot)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule is a contrapositive‑style relationship: a calibrated robot cannot be anticipatory. This is the key bridge that will let us turn an anticipatory conclusion about beta_bot into the desired non‑calibrated conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a robot is calibrated, then it cannot be anticipatory.</text>
<logic>compliant(beta_bot)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">

</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Beta_bot is anticipatory.</text>
<logic>anticipatory(beta_bot)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Beta_bot is not calibrated.</text>
<logic>~calibrated(beta_bot)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Beta_bot is not calibrated, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

His colleagues admire his ability to keep the team cohesive, often describing him as a true team_player.
examples.question: Given the information about master_carpenter, can we conclude that he is precise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether master_carpenter possesses the quality of being precise. To answer, we must follow the chain of given facts and logical rules that relate his systematic nature, negotiating skill, and teamwork orientation to precision.

The first rule links negotiator status to being a team_player. This connection will become useful later because once we establish that master_carpenter negotiates, we can immediately infer his teamwork quality, which is part of the argument about precision.

</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if someone is precise, they cannot be a team_player. This will let us use the presence of team_player to argue against precision through modus tollens later in the proof.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the master carpenter is precise, then he cannot be a team_player.</text>
<logic>~team_player(master_carpenter) :- precise(master_carpenter)</logic>

We are explicitly given that master_carpenter is systematic. This factual statement serves as the starting point for applying the rule that systematic implies negotiator, setting the deduction in motion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The master carpenter is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the systematic fact and the rule that systematic leads to negotiator, we deduce that master_carpenter is a negotiator. The reasoning is straightforward: the condition of the rule is satisfied, so its conclusion follows.

Now that we have established master_carpenter as a negotiator, we can invoke the first rule. Using modus ponens again, the negotiator status triggers the conclusion that he is a team_player, linking his negotiation skill to teamwork.

<logic>team_player(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

At this point we know master_carpenter is a team_player. The third rule states that a precise individual cannot be a team_player. By applying modus tollens—since the consequent (not team_player) is false—we infer that master_carpenter cannot be precise, giving us ~precise.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
