examples.context: The nutritionist has formulated a special protein shake called the protein_shake. It is celebrated for being protein_rich, which makes it a favorite among athletes. In this formulation, any shake that absorbs quickly is considered high_fiber, because rapid absorption often coincides with added fiber for sustained energy. Moreover, a shake that is high_fiber is always packaged with an exact_serving, ensuring that each scoop delivers a precise amount of nutrients. A logical statement also tells us that either the shake is not protein_rich or it is fast_absorbing. Given these relationships, we want to know whether the protein_shake indeed has an exact_serving.
examples.question: Does the protein_shake have an exact serving size?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the protein_shake possesses an exact_serving. To answer this, we must trace the logical connections from the known facts about the shake to the property of exact_serving.

The first rule links fast_absorbing to high_fiber. It will be useful later because if we can show the shake is fast_absorbing, we immediately obtain high_fiber, moving us toward the goal of exact_serving.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a protein shake absorbs quickly, then it is high in fiber.</text>
<logic>high_fiber(protein_shake) :- fast_absorbing(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects high_fiber to exact_serving. This is the final bridge in the chain: once we have high_fiber, we can infer the precise serving size we are trying to prove.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a protein shake is high in fiber, then its serving size is measured precisely.</text>
<logic>exact_serving(protein_shake) :- high_fiber(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either the shake is not protein_rich or it is fast_absorbing. This clause will allow us to derive fast_absorbing once we know the shake is protein_rich, because the first alternative will be ruled out.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the shake is not protein‑rich, or it absorbs quickly.</text>
<logic>~protein_rich(protein_shake) v fast_absorbing(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that the shake is protein_rich. This fact will interact with the disjunction to eliminate the ‘not protein_rich’ option and force the other side of the clause.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The shake is known to be protein‑rich.</text>
<logic>protein_rich(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

Applying disjunctive syllogism, we note that the shake being protein_rich makes the literal ‘not protein_rich’ false. Since the disjunction must be true, the remaining literal, fast_absorbing, must hold. Thus we derive that the protein_shake is fast_absorbing.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Since the shake is protein‑rich, the alternative ‘not protein‑rich’ is false, so the disjunction forces the shake to be fast‑absorbing.</text>
<logic>fast_absorbing(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have fast_absorbing, we can use modus ponens with the first rule (fast_absorbing → high_fiber). Because the antecedent is satisfied, the consequent follows, giving us that the shake is high_fiber.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because the shake absorbs quickly, we can conclude it is high in fiber.</text>
<logic>high_fiber(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

With high_fiber established, we invoke modus ponens on the second rule (high_fiber → exact_serving). The antecedent is true, so the consequent follows, concluding that the shake has an exact_serving.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given the shake is high in fiber, it follows that its serving size is measured precisely.</text>
<logic>exact_serving(protein_shake)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Protein_shake is exact serving, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the bustling intellectual salons of Alexandria, the philosopher Hypatia was famed for her keen mind and moral stature. Some accounts describe her as renowned among scholars, while others emphasize her valiant defense of learning against hostile forces. Observers also note that she could be zealous in her lectures, yet she always tried to remain neutral in civic disputes. Her students admired her sharp repartee, which often stemmed from a deep commitment to truth.
examples.question: Is it true that Hypatia is not learned?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the statement that Hypatia is not learned holds. To answer, we must examine the supplied facts and logical connections and see if they force Hypatia to be learned, which would make the negation false.

The first rule tells us that whenever Hypatia exhibits valiance, she must also be driven. This will let us turn a proof of valiance into a proof of being driven later in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Hypatia is valiant, then she is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(hypatia) :- valiant(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links neutrality to sharpness: if Hypatia is neutral, then she necessarily possesses a sharp wit. This direction will be useful once we establish neutrality.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Hypatia is neutral, then she is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(hypatia) :- neutral(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive style link: a zealous Hypatia cannot be sharp. Later we will use this to eliminate the possibility that she is zealous once we have shown she is sharp.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Hypatia is zealous, then she is not sharp.</text>
<logic>~sharp(hypatia) :- zealous(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of two conditions holds: Hypatia is zealous or she is learned. This will become a key source of information once we can rule out the zealous alternative.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Hypatia is zealous or she is learned.</text>
<logic>zealous(hypatia) v learned(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The fifth statement offers another disjunction: either Hypatia is not renowned or she is valiant. Since we already know she is renowned, the only way for the disjunction to be true is that she must be valiant.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Hypatia is not renowned or she is valiant.</text>
<logic>~renowned(hypatia) v valiant(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The sixth disjunction says that either Hypatia is not driven or she is neutral. When we later obtain that she is driven, the only way for this disjunction to stay true is to conclude that she is neutral.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Hypatia is not driven or she is neutral.</text>
<logic>~driven(hypatia) v neutral(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hypatia is renowned is a starting point for the reasoning. Combined with the disjunction in premise 5, it forces the other disjunct to be true, which is crucial for the next step.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Hypatia is renowned.</text>
<logic>renowned(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

Because Hypatia is renowned, the disjunction "not renowned or valiant" cannot be satisfied by the first option, so the second must hold, giving us that she is valiant. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism move, eliminating the impossible alternative.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>From her being renowned and the disjunction, we infer that Hypatia is valiant.</text>
<logic>valiant(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Hypatia is valiant, we apply the rule from premise 1, which says valiant implies driven. Thus we can directly infer that she is driven, moving the argument forward.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since Hypatia is valiant, the rule gives that she is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Hypatia is driven, we look at the disjunction "not driven or neutral". The first part is false because she is driven, so the only way the disjunction can be true is if she is neutral. Hence we deduce neutrality.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>With Hypatia driven and the disjunction, we conclude she is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

With neutrality in hand, we use the rule from premise 2: neutral leads to sharpness. This gives us that Hypatia possesses a sharp wit, a fact we will later contrast with the zealous condition.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because she is neutral, the rule yields that she is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

We have that Hypatia is sharp, and premise 3 tells us that if she were zealous she could not be sharp. Since she is sharp, the antecedent of that conditional must be false, allowing us to conclude that she is not zealous. This is a modus tollens step.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Given that she is sharp and the rule linking sharp to zealous, we deduce she is not zealous.</text>
<logic>~zealous(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from premise 4 says "zealous or learned". We have just shown she is not zealous, so the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is that she is learned. Therefore we infer that Hypatia is learned.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>From not zealous and the earlier disjunction, we infer that Hypatia is learned.</text>
<logic>learned(hypatia)</logic>
</step>

We derived Hypatia is learned, so the conclusion Hypatia is not learned is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The second premise offers a disjunction: either the karateka lacks strict training or he follows a systematic form. This structure is crucial because once we establish strict training, the only way for the disjunction to stay true is for the systematic form to be true.

The third premise gives us the concrete fact that the karateka practices diligently. This fact is the starting point for applying the conditional rule from Premise 1.

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact of diligent practice with the rule that diligence implies strict training. Because the antecedent holds, we can confidently assert that the karateka is strictly trained.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">

Therefore Young_karateka is systematic form, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the bustling workshop of Oak & Elm, carpenter_jordan is known for his meticulous approach to every project. He checks each joint, sands each surface, and never leaves a board unchecked. The shopmates talk about his flexibility with new designs, his sharp-eyed attention to detail, and how his methodical habits keep the workbench orderly. Although he sometimes feels satisfied with a finished piece, the atmosphere is competitive, with several woodworkers striving to outdo each other. Reliability and a love of learning from old carpentry manuals also shape his reputation among the crew.
examples.question: Given the information about carpenter_jordan, can we conclude that he is not a rival woodworker?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with a set of observations about carpenter_jordan's work habits and the relationships among various traits. The goal is to determine whether the workshop's competitive pressure makes him a rival woodworker, i.e., whether the predicate rival_woodworker applies to him.

The first rule tells us that meticulous work always goes hand‑in‑hand with flexibility in design choices. This link will let us move from a concrete fact about his meticulousness to a more abstract quality we can later combine with other rules.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If carpenter_jordan is meticulous, then he is flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible(carpenter_jordan) :- meticulous(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes that anyone who is well_read automatically becomes sharp_eyed, meaning they notice fine details. This will be useful later when we need to connect knowledge about his learning habits to his observational skill.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If carpenter_jordan is well_read, then he is sharp_eyed.</text>
<logic>sharp_eyed(carpenter_jordan) :- well_read(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that a methodical approach guarantees a sense of satisfaction with the work. We'll need this chain to eventually reach the well_read condition.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If carpenter_jordan is methodical, then he is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(carpenter_jordan) :- methodical(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule creates a loop: if someone feels satisfied, they must be well_read. This reciprocal relationship lets us travel forward and backward between satisfaction and learning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If carpenter_jordan is satisfied, then he is well_read.</text>
<logic>well_read(carpenter_jordan) :- satisfied(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides a negative consequence: if someone is reliable, they cannot be flexible. This contradiction will be pivotal when we later discover his flexibility, forcing us to reject reliability.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If carpenter_jordan is reliable, then he is not flexible.</text>
<logic>~flexible(carpenter_jordan) :- reliable(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule gives a negative link from being a rival woodworker to losing sharp‑eyed perception. By showing he is sharp‑eyed, we will be able to deny the rival status through contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If carpenter_jordan is a rival woodworker, then he is not sharp_eyed.</text>
<logic>~sharp_eyed(carpenter_jordan) :- rival_woodworker(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise offers a disjunction: either he is reliable or he is methodical. This choice will allow us to eliminate one alternative and affirm the other once we have evidence against reliability.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either carpenter_jordan is reliable or he is methodical.</text>
<logic>reliable(carpenter_jordan) v methodical(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

The fact that carpenter_jordan is meticulous supplies the concrete starting point for the chain of deductions. From here we can activate the rule about flexibility.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Carpenter_jordan is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that he is meticulous together with the rule that meticulousness implies flexibility, we directly infer that carpenter_jordan is flexible. This gives us a positive attribute that will later clash with the reliability rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From his meticulous nature we infer that carpenter_jordan is flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know he is flexible, the rule stating that reliability leads to non‑flexibility creates a conflict. By Modus Tollens, if flexibility holds, the antecedent (reliability) must be false, so we conclude he is not reliable.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Because he is flexible, we can reject that he is reliable, concluding he is not reliable.</text>
<logic>~reliable(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Having established that he is not reliable, the disjunction "reliable or methodical" cannot be satisfied by the first option. Therefore, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is that carpenter_jordan must be methodical. This uses Disjunctive Syllogism to pick the remaining alternative.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Since he is not reliable and we know either reliable or methodical holds, we deduce that carpenter_jordan is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

From his methodical nature, the rule that methodical implies satisfaction lets us deduce that he is satisfied. This step moves us forward along the chain linking work habits to emotional states.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>His methodical habit gives us that he is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Because he is satisfied, the reciprocal rule that satisfaction leads to being well_read lets us infer that carpenter_jordan is well_read. This completes the loop needed to reach his observational skill.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Being satisfied lets us infer that carpenter_jordan is well_read.</text>
<logic>well_read(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Having established he is well_read, the rule that well_read implies sharp_eyed lets us conclude that he is sharp_eyed. This attribute is crucial for the final negation of rival status.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>From being well_read we conclude that carpenter_jordan is sharp_eyed.</text>
<logic>sharp_eyed(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we know he is sharp_eyed, and the rule connecting rival woodworker status to a lack of sharp_eyed perception gives us a contrapositive: if someone is sharp_eyed, they cannot be a rival woodworker. Thus we infer that carpenter_jordan is not a rival woodworker.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>Because he is sharp_eyed, we can deny that he is a rival woodworker, establishing that he is not a rival woodworker.</text>
<logic>~rival_woodworker(carpenter_jordan)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Carpenter_jordan is not rival woodworker, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Harley is a field geologist who often works on stratigraphic sections. When a rock unit is layered, it tends to be stable under stress. A mineral‑rich exposure can make a geologist feel active about the next sampling. Logical reasoning about the terrain usually leads Harley to be surveyed before any detailed work. A stable outcrop is likely to be dormant in terms of further deformation. If Harley is ancient, the geologist will not act enthusiastically, and if a geologist is studied, the preparation might be called into question.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Harley is not dormant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with a set of geological style rules describing how different attributes of Harley relate to each other. Our goal is to see whether the chain of implications ultimately forces Harley to be dormant, and then to negate that claim.

The first premise tells us that layering leads to stability, which will be useful later when we try to derive dormancy. Recognizing this conditional helps us plan to later combine it with a derived layered fact.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Harley is layered, then Harley is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(harley) :- layered(harley)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links mineral richness to activity, giving us a route from a positive property to an energetic one. This will become relevant once we establish whether Harley is mineral‑rich.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Harley is mineral‑rich, then Harley is active.</text>
<logic>active(harley) :- mineral_rich(harley)</logic>
</step>

The third premise says logical thinking causes Harley to be surveyed, establishing a direct bridge from the known fact of logicalness. This bridge will allow us to generate a concrete surveyed fact.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Harley is logical, then Harley is surveyed.</text>
<logic>surveyed(harley) :- logical(harley)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise connects stability to dormancy, showing how a stable outcrop can be considered dormant. This rule will later let us turn a derived stable status into the target property of dormancy.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Harley is stable, then Harley is dormant.</text>
<logic>dormant(harley) :- stable(harley)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise states that an ancient geologist prevents activity, giving us a way to produce a negative activity statement from an ancient status. This will be used when we later obtain a contradiction involving activity.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Harley is ancient, then Harley is not active.</text>
<logic>~active(harley) :- ancient(harley)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise provides a contrapositive style link: if Harley were studied, then surveying would fail. This will later help us eliminate the possibility that Harley is studied.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Harley is studied, then Harley is not surveyed.</text>
<logic>~surveyed(harley) :- studied(harley)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise offers a disjunction: Harley must be either studied or mineral‑rich. This binary choice will be crucial once we rule out one of the options.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Harley is either studied or mineral‑rich.</text>
<logic>studied(harley) v mineral_rich(harley)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise gives another disjunction: Harley is either ancient or layered. This will let us decide between these two traits after we obtain information about ancientness.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Harley is either ancient or layered.</text>
<logic>ancient(harley) v layered(harley)</logic>
</step>

The ninth premise is a concrete fact: Harley is logical. This starting point will trigger the rule that logicalness leads to being surveyed.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Harley is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(harley)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that logicalness implies surveyed status, we directly infer that Harley is surveyed. This moves us from a known fact to a new attribute that can interact with other rules.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Harley being logical we infer that Harley is surveyed.</text>
<logic>surveyed(harley)</logic>
</step>

We now have a surveyed Harley, but the rule about studied Harley says a studied individual cannot be surveyed. Since we have a surveyed case, the only way to avoid contradiction is to conclude Harley cannot be studied. This negation will be used in the upcoming disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Because a surveyed Harley would contradict the rule that a studied Harley is not surveyed, we infer that Harley is not studied.</text>
<logic>~studied(harley)</logic>

The disjunction from premise 7 says Harley is either studied or mineral‑rich. Because we have just shown Harley is not studied, the only remaining option is that Harley is mineral‑rich. This resolves the disjunction in favor of mineral richness.

<text>Since Harley cannot be studied, the disjunction forces Harley to be mineral‑rich.</text>

With Harley established as mineral‑rich, premise 2 lets us infer that Harley is active. This gives us a positive activity fact that will soon clash with the ancient‑activity rule.

Premise 5 tells us that an ancient Harley would prevent activity. Since we just derived that Harley is active, the only way to keep the system consistent is to reject the ancient possibility, yielding Harley is not ancient. This negation will be fed into the second disjunction.

Finally, with Harley stable, premise 4 tells us that stability leads to dormancy. Thus we derive that Harley is dormant, completing the forward chain of implications.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">

examples.context: Socratic, a well‑known figure in ancient philosophy, is celebrated for his razor‑sharp rational mind. His followers often remark that a rational thinker tends to adopt a methodical way of organizing arguments. Moreover, a methodical philosopher is frequently described as altruistic, because careful planning allows one to consider the welfare of others. In debates about moral character, it is also claimed that either a philosopher is not altruistic or, if not, he must be deeply committed to his principles.
examples.question: Is Socratic committed?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Socratic, the philosophical figure, can be said to be committed. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the given facts and rules to reach a conclusion about his commitment.

The first rule tells us that methodicalness guarantees altruism. This will be useful later when we manage to establish that Socratic is methodical, allowing us to infer his altruistic character.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Socratic is methodical, then he is altruistic.</text>
<logic>altruistic(socratic) :- methodical(socratic)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects rationality with methodicalness. Since we already know Socratic is rational, this rule offers a direct route to claim he is methodical, which is a crucial intermediate step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Socratic is rational, then he is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(socratic) :- rational(socratic)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of two possibilities holds: Socratic is not altruistic, or he is committed. This logical form will enable us to eliminate the first alternative once we have proven Socratic is altruistic.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Socratic is not altruistic or Socratic is committed.</text>
<logic>~altruistic(socratic) v committed(socratic)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Socratic is rational provides the starting point for the deduction. It supplies the antecedent needed for the second rule, allowing us to move forward in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Socratic is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(socratic)</logic>
</step>

Applying the second rule to the fact that Socratic is rational, we deduce that Socratic must be methodical. This follows because the rule asserts that rationality always leads to methodicalness, and we have the rationality premise.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because Socratic is rational and rationality implies methodicalness, Socratic is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(socratic)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Socratic is methodical, we can invoke the first rule, which says methodicalness entails altruism. Consequently, Socratic is concluded to be altruistic, linking the earlier steps together.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Socratic is methodical and methodicalness leads to altruism, Socratic is altruistic.</text>
<logic>altruistic(socratic)</logic>
</step>

With Socratic confirmed as altruistic, the first part of the disjunction (~altruistic) is false. In a disjunction, if one disjunct is false, the other must be true; therefore, Socratic must be committed. This uses the principle of disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the impossible alternative.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given Socratic is altruistic and the disjunction that Socratic is not altruistic or committed, we conclude Socratic is committed.</text>
<logic>committed(socratic)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Socratic is committed, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

<logic>~experienced(galileo_probe) v high_resolution(galileo_probe)</logic>

Applying disjunctive syllogism, we combine the fact that the probe is experienced with the disjunction "not experienced or high_resolution". Because the "not experienced" part is false, the remaining alternative must be true, so the probe must have high_resolution capability.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">

<logic>high_resolution(galileo_probe)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have derived high_resolution for the probe, we can use the conditional rule from PREMISE_1. Since high_resolution implies mission_focused, and we have established high_resolution, we can infer that the probe is mission_focused.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Given that the probe has high_resolution capabilities and that such capabilities imply being mission_focused, we infer the probe is mission_focused.</text>
<logic>mission_focused(galileo_probe)</logic>
</step>

We derived Galileo_probe is mission focused, so the conclusion Galileo_probe is not mission focused is False.

examples.context: In the coastal research lab, a particular sea turtle has been observed for its behavioral traits. The animal is noted for being precise in its navigation, often following exact routes through the reef. Researchers also track whether the turtle responds quickly to stimuli, explores new areas, moves directionally, displays keen perception, and exhibits a comprehensive scanning of its surroundings. Various logical relationships among these traits have been documented by the marine biologists.
examples.question: Based on the given facts and rules, is the sea turtle exploratory?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several behavioral properties of a sea turtle and logical connections among them. The task is to determine whether, after applying those connections, the turtle can be said to be exploratory.

This premise provides the first conditional link we will need: precision implies responsiveness. It will allow us to move from the known precise fact to a new property later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a sea turtle is precise, then it is responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(sea_turtle) :- precise(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

Here we obtain a second conditional that ties keenness to directionality. It will become useful once we manage to derive keenness from other information.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a sea turtle is keen, then it is directional.</text>
<logic>directional(sea_turtle) :- keen(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects responsiveness to being comprehensive. Once we have responsiveness, this rule will let us infer a broader scanning behavior.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a sea turtle is responsive, then it is comprehensive.</text>
<logic>comprehensive(sea_turtle) :- responsive(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

This conditional tells us that if the turtle were exploratory, it could not be directional. It sets up a relationship that will be used in a contrapositive argument later.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a sea turtle is exploratory, then it is not directional.</text>
<logic>~directional(sea_turtle) :- exploratory(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

The disjunctive statement offers two alternatives: either the turtle is not comprehensive or it is keen. When we later establish comprehensiveness, we will be forced to accept the keen alternative.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either the sea turtle is not comprehensive or it is keen.</text>
<logic>~comprehensive(sea_turtle) v keen(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the sea turtle is precise is the starting point of the chain. It gives us a concrete truth from which we can trigger the first conditional rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a known fact that the sea turtle is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the turtle is precise (Premise 6) and we have the rule that precision leads to responsiveness (Premise 1), we can directly infer responsiveness. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From the fact that the sea turtle is precise and the rule linking precision to responsiveness, we infer that the sea turtle is responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

Having just derived that the turtle is responsive, we can now use the rule linking responsiveness to comprehensiveness (Premise 3). By applying modus ponens again, we obtain that the turtle is comprehensive.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Using the newly obtained responsiveness and the rule that responsiveness leads to being comprehensive, we infer that the sea turtle is comprehensive.</text>
<logic>comprehensive(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

We now possess the comprehensive property, which makes the left side of the disjunction in Premise 5 false. Since a disjunction is true when at least one side holds, the only way for the statement to remain true is for the right side, keenness, to be true. Thus we conclude the turtle is keen.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Having established that the sea turtle is comprehensive, the disjunction tells us that the alternative must be false, so the sea turtle must be keen.</text>
<logic>keen(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

With keenness established, we can activate the rule that keenness implies directionality (Premise 2). Using modus ponens, we infer that the turtle is directional.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Since the sea turtle is keen, the rule that keenness yields directionality lets us conclude the sea turtle is directional.</text>
<logic>directional(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

We have shown the turtle is directional, but Premise 4 tells us that an exploratory turtle would be not directional. Applying modus tollens, the truth of directionality forces the negation of the exploratory condition, so the turtle cannot be exploratory.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Knowing the sea turtle is directional and that exploratory status would make it not directional, we apply modus tollens to infer that the sea turtle cannot be exploratory.</text>
<logic>~exploratory(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sea_turtle is not exploratory, so the conclusion Sea_turtle is exploratory is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Master carpenter Skyler has spent decades perfecting his craft, and his reputation for experience is unmatched in the workshop. Because of his seasoned expertise, the designs he sketches tend to feel instinctively right, guiding the joinery without a second thought. Yet the foremen often argue that a design that isn’t intuitive must be compensated for by a structure that is especially robust. The question on everyone's mind is whether Skyler's latest cabinet will stand out for its strength.
examples.question: Based on the given information, does Skyler's work exhibit strong construction?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the master carpenter's output qualifies as strong construction. To answer, we must trace how the given facts and logical rules lead from Skyler's experience to the property of a strong build.

The rule linking experience to intuitive design tells us that any seasoned carpenter automatically creates designs that feel natural. This connection will allow us to move from the fact that Skyler is experienced to a statement about his designs, which is essential for later reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a carpenter is experienced, then his designs are intuitive.</text>
<logic>design_intuitive(master_carpenter) :- experienced(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The disjunctive statement offers two alternatives: either the design lacks intuitiveness, or the final product is robust. Since we will eventually obtain an intuitive design, this clause will let us eliminate the first option and infer the second, giving us the desired strong_build property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either the design is not intuitive or the construction is strong.</text>
<logic>~design_intuitive(master_carpenter) v strong_build(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Skyler is experienced grounds the whole argument in a concrete premise. Without this factual anchor, we could not apply the rule from Premise 1, so it is a necessary starting point for the deduction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Skyler is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Skyler is experienced (Premise 3) and the rule says experience guarantees an intuitive design (Premise 1), we can apply Modus Ponens to conclude that Skyler's designs are indeed intuitive. This derived fact bridges the gap between the raw experience fact and the condition needed for the disjunction.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From Skyler being experienced and the rule, we infer that his designs are intuitive.</text>
<logic>design_intuitive(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Now we have the intuitive design statement from Step 4, and we also have the disjunction that either the design is not intuitive or the construction is strong (Premise 2). Since the first disjunct is false—our design is intuitive—we can invoke Disjunctive Syllogism to discard it and accept the second disjunct, concluding that the construction is strong.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Using the intuitive design and the disjunction, we conclude that his construction is strong.</text>
<logic>strong_build(master_carpenter)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Master_carpenter is strong build, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the university robotics lab, three graduate assistants each bring a distinctive set of qualities to the team. <step n="9" type="premise">
<text>If Carl is creative, then Carl is not systematic.</text>

<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,8">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Carl is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(carl)</logic>

<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Carl is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(carl)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Carl is not sharp.</text>
<logic>~sharp(carl)</logic>
</step>

<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Carl is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(carl)</logic>
</step>

<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Carl is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(carl)</logic>
</step>

<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Carl is not creative.</text>
<logic>~creative(carl)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Carl is scholarly. The disjunction provided states that either Carl is not scholarly or he is strong. Since the first part of the disjunction is false (Carl is indeed scholarly), the only way for the disjunction to be true is for the second part to hold, so Carl must be strong. This uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the impossible alternative.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,1">
<text>From Carl being scholarly and the disjunction that he is either not scholarly or strong, we infer that Carl is strong.</text>
<logic>adaptable(carl)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Carl's strength, we can apply the conditional rule linking strength to determination. The rule says that if someone is strong, then they are determined, so Carl inherits the property of being determined. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,6">
<text>Because Carl is strong, the rule linking strength to determination lets us conclude that Carl is determined.</text>
<logic>insightful(carl)</logic>
</step>

We derived Carl is insightful, so the conclusion Carl is not insightful is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is it true that Walter is not productive?

examples.context: The Silver Stream pours into the coastal basin, carrying fresh water and a host of dynamic qualities. Scientists have observed that its currents are often turbulent, that it exhibits strong upwelling, and that its salinity level can be traced back to earlier energetic behavior. They also note that when the stream is adaptive, its salinity tends to diminish, while the stream’s drive is evident from its persistent flow. Additionally, measurements show that either the stream is adaptive or it performs deep‑diving maneuvers as it meets the ocean.
examples.question: Is it true that the Silver Stream is not deep‑diving?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement "the Silver Stream is not deep‑diving" holds. The goal is to trace the chain of properties from the given facts and rules to see if the opposite—being deep‑diving—must follow.

The rule linking upwelling to turbulence tells us that any observation of upwelling automatically grants turbulence. This will be useful once we obtain upwelling for the stream.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the stream shows upwelling, it can be considered turbulent.</text>
<logic>turbulent(silver_stream) :- upwelling(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that a driven stream inevitably shows upwelling. Since we already have the fact that the Silver Stream is driven, this rule gives us a path to upwelling.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the stream is driven, then it necessarily displays upwelling.</text>
<logic>upwelling(silver_stream) :- driven(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

According to the third rule, turbulence implies salinity. Once we can prove the stream is turbulent, we will be able to assert it is saline.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being turbulent guarantees that the stream is saline.</text>
<logic>saline(silver_stream) :- turbulent(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if the stream is adaptive, it cannot be saline. This will later let us eliminate adaptiveness once we know the stream is saline.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the stream is adaptive, then it cannot be saline.</text>
<logic>~saline(silver_stream) :- adaptive(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction captures a choice: the stream is either adaptive or it performs deep‑diving. This binary will be decisive after we rule out one of the options.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either the stream is adaptive or it performs deep‑diving.</text>
<logic>adaptive(silver_stream) v deep_diving(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the Silver Stream is driven is our starting point. It directly activates the rule that a driven stream upwells, setting the chain in motion.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The Silver Stream is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

Using the driven fact together with the rule that driven implies upwelling, we conclude the Silver Stream exhibits upwelling. This new fact is essential for applying the turbulence rule next.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From its driven nature we infer that the Silver Stream shows upwelling.</text>
<logic>upwelling(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have upwelling, the rule connecting upwelling to turbulence lets us infer that the stream is turbulent. This step bridges the earlier property to salinity.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because it upwells, we conclude that the stream is turbulent.</text>
<logic>turbulent(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

With turbulence established, the rule stating turbulence leads to salinity allows us to assert the Silver Stream is saline. This property will be pivotal for eliminating adaptiveness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Having established turbulence, we deduce that the stream is saline.</text>
<logic>saline(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

Because the stream is saline, the rule that adaptive streams cannot be saline forces the negation of adaptiveness. Hence we derive that the Silver Stream is not adaptive.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since the stream is saline, we infer that it cannot be adaptive.</text>
<logic>~adaptive(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

Having shown the stream is not adaptive, the disjunction (adaptive or deep‑diving) leaves only the deep‑diving alternative viable. Therefore we deduce the Silver Stream is deep‑diving.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that it is not adaptive, the disjunction forces the stream to be deep‑diving.</text>
<logic>deep_diving(silver_stream)</logic>
</step>

We derived Silver_stream is deep diving, so the conclusion Silver_stream is not deep diving is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Dr. Patel is treating a senior horse named Miles at the clinic. The veterinary notes state that Miles is fair‑minded, which often indicates a calm temperament. In this practice, a fair‑minded animal that can express itself clearly tends to be eloquent, and eloquence is linked with courage in the herd hierarchy. Courage, in turn, usually makes a horse agile on the pasture. Separately, an intuitive horse is considered humble, while an articulate horse is never agile. The staff also observed that either Miles is articulate or he is intuitive, but they could not decide which at first glance.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we conclude that Miles is not humble?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the claim that Miles is not humble can be supported by the given veterinary facts and rules. The goal is to see if the knowledge base forces humility on Miles, which would make the negated claim false.

The first rule connects courage to agility, telling us that any courageous horse automatically gains agility. This link will be useful later because we will eventually establish Miles's courage and need to translate it into a concrete property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Miles is courageous, then he must be agile.</text>
<logic>agile(miles) :- courageous(miles)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that eloquence implies courage. By chaining this with the previous rule, we can move from an eloquent description of Miles all the way to agility, should we manage to prove eloquence first.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Miles is eloquent, then he must be courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(miles) :- eloquent(miles)</logic>
</step>

The third rule gives a direct path from the known fact that Miles is fair‑minded to the property of being eloquent. This is the starting point for the chain that will eventually lead to humility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Miles is fair‑minded, then he must be eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(miles) :- fair_minded(miles)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that intuition leads to humility. Since humility is the predicate we care about, establishing that Miles is intuitive will directly give us the desired conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Miles is intuitive, then he must be humble.</text>
<logic>humble(miles) :- intuitive(miles)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides a negative connection: if Miles were articulate, he could not be agile. This will become a crucial piece when we later discover that Miles is indeed agile, allowing us to rule out articulation.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Miles is articulate, then he cannot be agile.</text>
<logic>~agile(miles) :- articulate(miles)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of the two properties—articulate or intuitive—holds for Miles. This binary choice will be resolved once we eliminate one of the options using other information.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Miles is articulate or Miles is intuitive.</text>
<logic>articulate(miles) v intuitive(miles)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Miles is fair‑minded is the only concrete observation we start with. All other properties must be derived from this datum via the rules supplied.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Miles is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(miles)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens to the fact that Miles is fair‑minded and the rule that fair‑mindedness implies eloquence, we can directly infer that Miles is eloquent. This moves us one step forward along the chain toward courage and agility.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From Miles being fair‑minded we infer that he is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(miles)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established eloquence, we again use Modus Ponens with the rule linking eloquence to courage. Consequently, Miles is courageous, giving us another link in the chain needed for later deductions.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Miles is eloquent, we can deduce that he is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(miles)</logic>
</step>

With courage in hand, the first rule lets us infer agility through another application of Modus Ponens. At this point we know Miles is agile, which will be instrumental for eliminating the articulate alternative.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given Miles's courage, we conclude that he is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(miles)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know Miles is agile, the rule that an articulate horse would make him non‑agile creates a contradiction if Miles were articulate. By Modus Tollens, we must conclude that Miles cannot be articulate, thereby narrowing the disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Miles is agile, the rule that an articulate horse would make him non‑agile forces us to reject the possibility that he is articulate.</text>
<logic>~articulate(miles)</logic>
</step>

Having ruled out the articulate side of the disjunction, the only remaining possibility is that Miles is intuitive. Disjunctive Syllogism lets us safely assert his intuition, which is the missing link to humility.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>With the articulation option ruled out, the disjunction tells us that Miles must be intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(miles)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that intuition leads to humility lets us apply Modus Ponens one last time. Because Miles is intuitive, we can directly conclude that he is humble.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>If Miles is intuitive, the earlier rule gives us that he is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(miles)</logic>
</step>

We derived Miles is humble, so the conclusion Miles is not humble is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In a linguistic analysis, the researcher inspects a particular syntax tree to understand how its structural properties affect the style of the sentence it represents. The tree can be described as complex, neutral, logical, verbose, or concise, each reflecting a different facet of linguistic expression. By tracing the relationships among these descriptors, the analyst hopes to determine whether the sentence conveyed by the tree is concise in its wording.
examples.question: Based on the given information, does the syntax tree exhibit conciseness?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether a particular syntax tree can be described as concise. To answer, we must follow the chain of logical relationships supplied in the premises and derive the desired property step by step.

This premise connects verbosity with conciseness, which will be crucial later when we finally establish that the tree is verbose. Knowing that verbose implies concise gives us a direct route to the goal once we have verbosity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the syntax tree is verbose, then it is concise.</text>
<logic>concise(syntax_tree) :- verbose(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that complexity guarantees neutrality. Since we already have a fact about the tree's complexity, this rule will let us move from a known property to a new one that participates in later inferences.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that a complex syntax tree is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(syntax_tree) :- complex(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that neutrality leads to logicality. Once we have shown the tree is neutral, we can immediately infer it is logical, setting up the use of the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>According to the rule, a neutral syntax tree is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(syntax_tree) :- neutral(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: the tree is either not logical or it is verbose. When we later confirm the tree is logical, this statement will force the second alternative—verbosity—to hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the syntax tree is not logical or it is verbose.</text>
<logic>~logical(syntax_tree) v verbose(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the syntax tree is complex provides the starting point for our derivation. It is the only concrete property given, and it will trigger the rule that produces neutrality.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The syntax tree is complex.</text>
<logic>complex(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that the tree is complex with the rule that complexity implies neutrality, we deduce the tree is neutral. This step is essential because neutrality is the premise needed for the next rule that yields logicality.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the complexity of the syntax tree and the rule linking complexity to neutrality, we infer that the syntax tree is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

Having established neutrality, we apply the rule linking neutrality to logicality, concluding that the tree is logical. This logical status will interact with the disjunction to produce verbosity.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Using the neutrality of the syntax tree and the rule that neutrality leads to logical, we conclude the syntax tree is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

We now have the tree being logical, and the disjunction tells us that either it is not logical or it is verbose. Since the first option is false, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for the tree to be verbose. Thus we infer verbosity.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Since the syntax tree is logical and we have the disjunction that either it is not logical or verbose, we resolve that it must be verbose.</text>
<logic>verbose(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

With verbosity secured, we invoke the earlier rule that verbosity guarantees conciseness. This directly yields the desired property that the syntax tree is concise.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given the syntax tree is verbose and the rule that verbosity implies conciseness, we deduce the syntax tree is concise.</text>
<logic>concise(syntax_tree)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Syntax_tree is concise, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the annals of antiquity the great city‑states of Alexandria, Babylon, Carthage and Thebes each earned a reputation for their distinct virtues. Scholars record that Alexandria, Babylon, Carthage and Thebes were all celebrated for their wisdom. Babylon, Carthage and Thebes, however, were never described as zealous in the historical chronicles. Various maxims guided the interpretation of these traits: a city that is wise is deemed valiant; a city that lacks zeal is considered tolerant; valiance leads to endurance; strategic insight brings tranquility; and a zealous city cannot be enduring. Moreover, a single ancient proverb states that either a city is not tolerant or it is strategic. The question asks whether Alexandria can be said to lack tranquility.
examples.question: Is it true that Alexandria is not tranquil?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the ancient city of Alexandria lacks tranquility. To answer, we must follow the chain of logical relationships supplied about wisdom, valiance, endurance, tolerance, strategy, and tranquility.

The first rule tells us that wisdom implies valiance. This will let us turn the known fact that Alexandria is wise into a statement about its bravery, a crucial link for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a city is wise, then it is valiant.</text>
<logic>valiant(alexandria) :- wise(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links the absence of zeal to tolerance. Since several cities are noted as not zealous, this rule will later allow us to infer tolerance for any city we can show is not zealous.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a city is not zealous, then it is tolerant.</text>
<logic>enduring(alexandria) :- valiant(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that valiance leads to endurance. Once we have established Alexandria's valiance, we can immediately claim it endures, moving the proof forward toward the goal.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a city is valiant, then it is enduring.</text>
<logic>tranquil(alexandria) :- strategic(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule connects strategic insight with tranquility. This means that any city we can prove strategic will automatically be tranquil, which directly bears on the query about Alexandria's tranquility.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a city is strategic, then it is tranquil.</text>
<logic>~enduring(alexandria) :- zealous(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if a city is zealous, it cannot be enduring. This will be useful for deriving a non‑zealous condition from an already known enduring status.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a city is zealous, then it is not enduring.</text>
<logic>~tolerant(alexandria) v strategic(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

The disjunctive statement offers two alternatives: either a city is not tolerant or it is strategic. This logical fork will let us obtain strategic status once we have established tolerance for Alexandria.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either a city is not tolerant or it is strategic.</text>
<logic>wise(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>By Universal Instantiation, grounding the quantified rule for this entity gives Alexandria is valiant.</text>
<logic>valiant(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that wisdom implies valiance to the fact that Alexandria is wise gives us the conclusion that Alexandria is valiant. This is a direct use of modus ponens, turning a general rule into a specific instance.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Alexandria being wise we infer that Alexandria is valiant.</text>
<logic>enduring(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

With Alexandria now known to be valiant, we invoke the rule linking valiance to endurance. Consequently, Alexandria is concluded to be enduring, progressing us toward the non‑zealous condition needed later.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Given Alexandria's valiance, we conclude that Alexandria is enduring.</text>
<logic>~zealous(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

Since we have established Alexandria's endurance, we can employ the contrapositive rule that a zealous city cannot be enduring. The presence of endurance forces the negation of zeal, so we infer that Alexandria is not zealous.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9">
<text>Because Alexandria is enduring, we deduce that Alexandria is not zealous.</text>
<logic>tolerant(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Alexandria is not zealous, we can use the rule that a lack of zeal yields tolerance. This step converts the non‑zealous status into the attribute of tolerance for Alexandria.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>From Alexandria not being zealous, we obtain that Alexandria is tolerant.</text>
<logic>strategic(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

Now that Alexandria is tolerant, we turn to the disjunction "not tolerant or strategic". Because the first disjunct is false (Alexandria is tolerant), the disjunction forces the second disjunct to be true, so Alexandria must be strategic.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Combining Alexandria's tolerance with the disjunction yields that Alexandria is strategic.</text>
<logic>tranquil(alexandria)</logic>
</step>

We derived Alexandria is tranquil, so the conclusion Alexandria is not tranquil is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: At the downtown redevelopment project, engineers are evaluating two key structural elements: a steel beam that will span the main thoroughfare and a concrete column that will support the new office tower. examples.question: Is the concrete column cost‑efficient?

<think>
We need to decide whether the concrete column can be described as cost‑efficient. The goal is to see if the predicate cost_efficient(concrete_column) holds, given the facts and rules about code compliance and load‑bearing status.

The first rule links code compliance to load‑bearing capability, which is crucial because we know the column is code‑compliant. Recognizing this connection lets us later deduce the column's structural role.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a concrete column meets all building codes, then it is considered load‑bearing.</text>
<logic>load_bearing(concrete_column) :- code_compliant(concrete_column)</logic>
</step>

The second rule creates a tension between cost efficiency and being load‑bearing; it tells us that a cost‑efficient column must not be load‑bearing. This conditional will be used to eliminate the possibility of cost efficiency once we establish load‑bearing.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a concrete column is cost‑efficient, then it cannot be load‑bearing.</text>
<logic>~load_bearing(concrete_column) :- cost_efficient(concrete_column)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the concrete column satisfies all building codes gives us a concrete starting point. This fact directly activates the first rule, allowing us to infer additional properties about the column.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The concrete column in question satisfies all applicable building codes.</text>
<logic>code_compliant(concrete_column)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens, we combine the fact that the column is code‑compliant with the rule that code compliance implies load‑bearing. Because the antecedent of the rule is true, we can confidently assert that the column is load‑bearing.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the code‑compliance of the column and the first rule, we infer that the column is load‑bearing.</text>
<logic>load_bearing(concrete_column)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the column is load‑bearing, we turn to the second rule, which states that a cost‑efficient column cannot be load‑bearing. Using Modus Tollens, the presence of load‑bearing forces the negation of cost efficiency, so we conclude the column is not cost‑efficient.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the column is load‑bearing, the second rule tells us that it cannot be cost‑efficient, so we conclude it is not cost‑efficient.</text>
<logic>~cost_efficient(concrete_column)</logic>
</step>

We derived Concrete_column is not cost efficient, so the conclusion Concrete_column is cost efficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the grand opera house, three dancers—Juliet, Isadora, and Vaslav—are renowned for their artistry. Juliet and Isadora are praised for their expressive performances, while Vaslav is celebrated for his creativity on stage. The company keeps meticulous records of each dancer's qualities: whether they are strong, exploratory, devoted, balanced, agile, expressive, creative, or rehearsed. Training logs show how one quality often leads to another, and occasional contradictions appear in the notes, especially regarding the balance of a dancer's temperament.
examples.question: Based on the recorded relationships among the dancers' qualities, can we conclude that Vaslav is not balanced in his approach?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Vaslav lacks the quality of being balanced. To answer, we must trace the chain of properties recorded for him, using the given rules and the disjunctive statement about his exploratory nature and balance. By systematically applying each rule, we can see what conclusions follow about his balance.

This rule tells us that creativity always brings strength. It will be useful later when we establish Vaslav's creativity, allowing us to claim he is strong. Knowing that strength leads to other traits helps build the chain toward the balance question.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever a dancer is creative, they are also strong.</text>
<logic>strong(vaslav) :- creative(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that a lack of devotion forces a dancer to be exploratory. This conditional will become relevant once we determine whether Vaslav is devoted or not, because it will give us a direct route to his exploratory status, which appears in the disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a dancer is not devoted, then they are exploratory.</text>
<logic>agile(vaslav) :- expressive(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects expressiveness to agility. Since we already have Vaslav as expressive, this rule immediately gives us a new property—agility—that can be used to unlock further implications about his creativity and strength.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>A dancer who is expressive is automatically agile.</text>
<logic>creative(vaslav) :- agile(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that agility guarantees creativity. Once we have agility from the previous step, we can infer creativity, which in turn will activate the rule about strength. This creates a forward‑chaining link toward the trait of being rehearsed.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Being agile guarantees that the dancer is creative.</text>
<logic>rehearsed(vaslav) :- strong(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, any dancer who is strong must also be rehearsed. Once we establish Vaslav's strength, this rule will allow us to claim he is rehearsed, which will later be crucial for deriving a negation of devotion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a dancer is strong, then they are rehearsed.</text>
<logic>~rehearsed(vaslav) :- devoted(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

This rule provides a contradiction: a devoted dancer cannot be rehearsed. If we later find Vaslav rehearsed, we can use this rule contrapositively to infer that he cannot be devoted, which will feed into the exploratory rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>A dancer who is devoted cannot be rehearsed.</text>
<logic>~exploratory(vaslav) v balanced(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either Vaslav is not exploratory or he is balanced. This logical split will be resolved once we know Vaslav's exploratory status; if he is indeed exploratory, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that he must be balanced.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Vaslav is not exploratory or he is balanced.</text>
<logic>expressive(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>By Universal Instantiation, grounding the quantified rule for this entity gives Vaslav is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Vaslav is expressive together with the rule that expressiveness implies agility, we can safely assert that Vaslav is agile. This new property is the cornerstone for the next inference about his creativity.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>From Vaslav being expressive we infer that he is agile.</text>
<logic>creative(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

Given Vaslav's agility, the rule stating that agility leads to creativity lets us conclude that Vaslav is creative. Now we have a property that, via another rule, will give us strength.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Vaslav is agile, we deduce that he is creative.</text>
<logic>strong(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

Because we have established Vaslav's creativity, we can invoke the rule that creativity entails strength. This step is essential because strength will open the door to the rehearsed property, moving us closer to the contradiction about devotion.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Vaslav is creative, we conclude that he is strong.</text>
<logic>rehearsed(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

From Vaslav's strength we apply the rule that strength forces a dancer to be rehearsed. This yields the fact that Vaslav is rehearsed, which will be pivotal for the next step involving the devoted predicate.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given Vaslav's strength, we infer that he is rehearsed.</text>
<logic>~devoted(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

We now have Vaslav rehearsed, and the rule that a devoted dancer cannot be rehearsed allows us to infer the contrapositive: if a dancer is rehearsed, they cannot be devoted. Hence, Vaslav is not devoted. This negation will activate the exploratory rule.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12">
<text>From Vaslav being rehearsed we derive that he is not devoted.</text>
<logic>exploratory(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

Since Vaslav is not devoted, the rule that non‑devotion implies exploratory gives us that Vaslav is exploratory. This directly interacts with the disjunction we noted earlier, setting up the final resolution about balance.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Because Vaslav is not devoted, we infer that he is exploratory.</text>
<logic>balanced(vaslav)</logic>
</step>

We derived Vaslav is balanced, so the conclusion Vaslav is not balanced is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the bustling flight school, pilot Jamie and pilot Quinn are both known for their technical abilities. Jamie has demonstrated exceptional adeptness during simulator sessions, while Quinn, despite being equally adept, has occasionally shown a lack of readiness for certain missions. The instructors keep detailed records of each pilot's traits, such as logical thinking, positive mindset, and steady effort, to predict performance under pressure.
examples.question: Based on the recorded traits and the logical relationships among them, can we conclude that pilot Jamie is astute?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether pilot Jamie possesses the quality of being astute. The problem supplies a network of aviation‑related traits and logical connections, so our goal is to follow those connections step by step until we can either prove or refute Jamie's astuteness.

The first rule tells us that logical thinking implies a hardworking attitude. This will be useful later because we already know Jamie is logical, and we need to build a chain toward the astute property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is logical, then that pilot is also considered hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(pilot_jamie) :- logical(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links a positive mindset to being a sharp observer. It establishes a forward direction from optimism to insight, which we will activate once we derive Jamie's optimism.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>A pilot who maintains a positive mindset will be regarded as a sharp observer.</text>
<logic>sharp_observer(pilot_jamie) :- positive_mindset(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a bridge from being hardworking to adopting a positive mindset. It allows us to move from a work‑ethic characteristic to an emotional one, which is essential for the subsequent insight step.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being hardworking leads a pilot to develop a positive mindset.</text>
<logic>positive_mindset(pilot_jamie) :- hardworking(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

This rule states that a lack of flight readiness forces the instructor to note steady effort. It will become relevant after we determine Jamie's readiness status, helping us obtain the steady effort predicate needed for the final disjunction.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>When a pilot is not ready for flight, the instructor notes that the pilot shows steady effort.</text>
<logic>logical(pilot_jamie) :- adept(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule simply says that any adept pilot is automatically logical. Since we have a fact that Jamie is adept, this rule will let us infer Jamie's logical nature right away.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Adept pilots are automatically classified as logical.</text>
<logic>~sharp_observer(pilot_jamie) :- ready_for_flight(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, if a pilot is ready for flight, they cannot be a sharp observer. It provides a way to derive a negative readiness condition from the presence of insight, which we will exploit later.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is ready for flight, the instructor records that the pilot is not a sharp observer.</text>
<logic>~steady_effort(pilot_jamie) v astute(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either Jamie does not display steady effort, or Jamie is astute. This is the key step that will let us conclude astuteness once we can eliminate the first alternative.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Jamie does not show steady effort, or Jamie is astute.</text>
<logic>adept(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>By Universal Instantiation, grounding the quantified rule for this entity gives Pilot_jamie is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

By applying the rule that adept pilots are logical to the fact that Jamie is adept, we directly infer Jamie's logical status. This step transforms a concrete skill into an abstract reasoning ability, setting the stage for later deductions.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Jamie being adept and the rule that adept pilots are logical, we infer that Jamie is logical.</text>
<logic>hardworking(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Having established Jamie's logical nature, we now invoke the first premise: logical pilots are hardworking. This modus ponens application yields Jamie's hardworking attribute, moving us further along the causal chain.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since Jamie is logical and logical pilots are hardworking, Jamie becomes hardworking.</text>
<logic>positive_mindset(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

With Jamie now classified as hardworking, we can use the third premise, which tells us that hardworking pilots adopt a positive mindset. Consequently, Jamie acquires a positive mindset, bringing us closer to the insight predicate.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Jamie is now hardworking, the rule linking hardworking to a positive mindset gives Jamie a positive mindset.</text>
<logic>sharp_observer(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Given Jamie's positive mindset, the second rule activates, allowing us to deduce that Jamie is a sharp observer. This step translates an emotional state into an observational skill, which will later help us assess readiness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>With Jamie's positive mindset, the earlier rule tells us Jamie is a sharp observer.</text>
<logic>~ready_for_flight(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

We now possess Jamie's sharp observer status. The sixth rule asserts that a ready‑for‑flight pilot cannot be a sharp observer. By contraposition (modus tollens), the presence of insight forces the negation of readiness, so we conclude Jamie is not ready for flight.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12">
<text>Given that Jamie is a sharp observer and the rule that a ready‑for‑flight pilot cannot be a sharp observer, we deduce that Jamie is not ready for flight.</text>
<logic>steady_effort(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Since Jamie is not ready for flight, the fourth premise applies, converting the lack of readiness into steady effort. Thus, we obtain Jamie's steady effort attribute, which directly addresses the first alternative in the disjunction.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>From Jamie not being ready for flight and the rule that such pilots show steady effort, we obtain that Jamie displays steady effort.</text>
<logic>astute(pilot_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Pilot_jamie is astute, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the coastal reserve, researchers monitor several flagship species. The tiger_king prowls the mangroves, the forest_elephant roams the canopy, and the river_dolphin glides through the estuary. Each animal is evaluated for traits that aid conservation: being swift_swimmer, energy_efficient, habitat_respecting, or behavior_adaptable. The team records that the river_dolphin is behavior_adaptable, and they have a set of logical guidelines linking these traits together.
examples.question: Based on the recorded facts and the conservation guidelines, can we conclude that the river_dolphin is not swift_swimmer?
examples.completion:
<think>
The conservation team wants to assess whether the river_dolphin lacks the swift_swimmer capability, which would affect its ability to evade predators. To answer this, we must trace the logical connections among the recorded traits and the rules that bind them.

The first rule tells us that energy efficiency guarantees swiftness; this creates a direct bridge from a measurable efficiency to the target property. Keeping this in mind, any proof that the dolphin is energy efficient will immediately give us information about its swimming speed.
<step n="1" type="premise">

<logic>swift_swimmer(river_dolphin) :- energy_efficient(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links behavior adaptability to being habitat respecting, establishing that a flexible animal tends to respect its environment. This relationship will be useful because the dolphin is known to be behavior adaptable, allowing us to derive the respecting trait.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the river dolphin is behavior adaptable, then it is habitat respecting.</text>
<logic>habitat_respecting(river_dolphin) :- behavior_adaptable(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction captures a logical alternative: either the dolphin does not respect its habitat, or it is energy efficient. This statement will become pivotal once we know whether the dolphin respects its habitat, because it will force the other side of the ‘or’ to hold.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the river dolphin is not habitat respecting or it is energy efficient.</text>
<logic>~habitat_respecting(river_dolphin) v energy_efficient(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the river_dolphin is behavior adaptable gives us a concrete starting point. Since this property is already recorded, we can activate the rule that turns adaptability into habitat respect.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The river dolphin is behavior adaptable.</text>
<logic>behavior_adaptable(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_2 to the known behavior_adaptable fact, we infer that the dolphin is habitat_respecting. This step is straightforward: the condition of the rule is satisfied, so the conclusion follows directly.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because the dolphin is behavior adaptable, we infer that it is habitat respecting.</text>
<logic>habitat_respecting(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

Now we have habitat_respecting, which matches the positive literal in the disjunction from PREMISE_3. Because the disjunction states that either ¬habitat_respecting or energy_efficient holds, the truth of habitat_respecting eliminates the first option, compelling energy_efficient to be true. Thus we derive that the dolphin is energy efficient.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since the dolphin is habitat respecting, the disjunction forces it to be energy efficient.</text>
<logic>energy_efficient(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

With energy_efficient established, we can invoke the conditional from PREMISE_1. The antecedent of that rule is satisfied, so the consequent—that the dolphin is swift_swimmer—must also hold. This gives us a direct derivation of the swift_swimmer property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Being energy efficient allows us to conclude the dolphin is swift swimmer.</text>
<logic>swift_swimmer(river_dolphin)</logic>
</step>

We derived River_dolphin is swift swimmer, so the conclusion River_dolphin is not swift swimmer is False.
</think>
Answer: False
