examples.context: In the downtown public library, each staff member brings a unique set of qualities to the team. Reference Casey is known for being very active during the busy hours, while Archivist David is celebrated for his meticulous attention to detail. The library handbook lists several conditional expectations: any employee who is dedicated is expected to show an adventurous spirit when tackling new projects; an adventurous employee is considered knowledgeable about the collection; an active worker who is efficient helps the library run smoothly; efficiency often follows from being detail‑oriented; dedication arises from empathy, and empathy is encouraged when someone is active. Moreover, anyone who is detail‑oriented automatically gains the trait of being efficient. These relationships allow the manager to infer additional qualities from the ones that are directly observed.
examples.question: Based on the given facts and rules, can we conclude that Archivist David is not knowledgeable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether the library’s archivist David lacks the quality of being knowledgeable. To answer this, we must trace the chain of properties that start from the facts we know about David and follow the conditional rules provided in the handbook.

The first rule tells us that dedication implies adventurousness for any archivist. This is important because later steps will need an adventurous trait to reach knowledgeability, so we keep this connection in mind.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any archivist who is dedicated is also adventurous.</text>
<logic>adventurous(archivist_david) :- dedicated(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links adventurousness directly to being knowledgeable. Recognizing this bridge is crucial because once we can establish David as adventurous, we will automatically obtain the knowledgeability we are investigating.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever someone is adventurous, they are considered knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(archivist_david) :- adventurous(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule that makes an active person empathetic. Although it does not involve David yet, it will become relevant once we derive activity for him, allowing us to move further along the chain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a person is active, then they are empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(archivist_david) :- active(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

This rule states that activity leads to efficiency. Since efficiency is a stepping stone toward dedication in later rules, identifying activity will eventually help us derive the adventurous quality.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Being efficient follows from being active.</text>
<logic>active(archivist_david) :- efficient(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says empathy gives rise to dedication. Knowing this will let us connect empathy (once we have it) to dedication, and then to adventurousness via Premise 1.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Dedication is a consequence of empathy.</text>
<logic>dedicated(archivist_david) :- empathetic(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, anyone who is detail‑oriented is also efficient. This provides the first link from a fact about David (his detail‑orientation) to a later property (efficiency).
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Efficiency is granted to anyone who is detail‑oriented.</text>
<logic>efficient(archivist_david) :- detail_oriented(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that David is detail‑oriented serves as the foundation for the entire derivation. It is the only concrete piece of information about David, and all subsequent properties will be inferred from it.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Archivist David is detail‑oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

Because we know David is detail‑oriented, we can assert that there exists at least one detail‑oriented person in the library. The rule from Premise 6 then activates, allowing us to infer that this person—David—must also be efficient. This step bridges the factual premise to a new derived property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,6">
<text>From David's detail‑oriented status we infer that there exists a detail‑oriented staff member, which allows us to apply the rule that detail‑oriented people are efficient, so David is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

Having established that David is efficient, we turn to Premise 4, which tells us that efficiency follows from activity. By applying this rule in reverse (modus ponens), we conclude that David must be active. This new activity status will enable further inferences about empathy and dedication.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since David is efficient, the rule that efficiency follows from activity lets us conclude that David is active.</text>
<logic>active(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

With David now identified as active, Premise 3 becomes applicable: an active individual is empathetic. Consequently, David inherits the empathetic quality, moving us one step closer to the dedication needed for adventurousness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Because David is active, the rule linking activity to empathy tells us that David is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

David's empathy triggers Premise 5, which states that empathy results in dedication. Therefore, David becomes dedicated. This dedication is exactly what Premise 1 requires to infer adventurousness in the next step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>David's empathy triggers the rule that empathy leads to dedication, so David becomes dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

Since David is dedicated, Premise 1 lets us infer that he is adventurous. This is a pivotal moment because adventurousness is the direct antecedent for knowledgeability according to Premise 2.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Being dedicated activates the rule that dedication implies adventurousness, giving David the adventurous trait.</text>
<logic>adventurous(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using Premise 2, we see that David's adventurous nature guarantees that he is knowledgeable. At this point we have derived the very property whose negation was questioned, showing that David indeed possesses it.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Finally, David's adventurousness activates the rule that adventurous people are knowledgeable, so David is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(archivist_david)</logic>
</step>

We derived Archivist_david is knowledgeable, so the conclusion Archivist_david is not knowledgeable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the Dragon Lotus dojo, four practitioners—Cole, Lia, Quinn, and Zion—train under the watchful eye of Master Kai. Each student displays distinct traits: some are fiercely ambitious, others excel at strategic planning, and a few are renowned for natural talent. The dojo's philosophy holds that ambition can turn a fighter into a fierce competitor, while strategic thinking nurtures a goal‑oriented mindset that eventually reveals hidden talent. Occasionally, the senior instructors note logical relationships among these qualities, such as "if a student is strategic, they become goal‑oriented" or "a goal‑oriented student inevitably proves talented". A particular discussion arises about Lia, who is known for her strategic approach, and whether this makes her competitive in upcoming tournaments.
examples.question: Based on the given facts and rules, is Lia competitive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Lia, a strategic practitioner at the dojo, can be classified as competitive. To answer this, we must trace how the supplied facts and logical rules connect ambition, goal‑orientation, talent, and competitiveness for Lia.

The first rule tells us that ambition is sufficient for competitiveness. This link will be crucial later, because if we can show Lia is ambitious, we can immediately infer she is competitive.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Lia is ambitious, then she is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(lia) :- ambitious(lia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides a bridge from strategic behavior to a goal‑oriented mindset. Since Lia is known to be strategic, this rule gives us a pathway to derive further properties about her.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Lia is strategic, then she is goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(lia) :- strategic(lia)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that being goal‑oriented guarantees talent. Once we have established Lia as goal‑oriented, we can use this rule to obtain her talent status, which will interact with the disjunction later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Lia is goal‑oriented, then she is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(lia) :- goal_oriented(lia)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction captures a logical alternative: either Lia lacks talent or she is ambitious. This will allow us to perform a disjunctive syllogism once we know one side of the statement is false.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Lia is not talented, or Lia is ambitious.</text>
<logic>~talented(lia) v ambitious(lia)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Lia is strategic is a concrete observation from the dojo's training records. This fact activates the rule linking strategic to goal‑oriented, setting the chain of inference in motion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Lia has a strategic fighting style.</text>
<logic>strategic(lia)</logic>
</step>

By combining the fact that Lia is strategic (Premise 5) with the rule that strategic implies goal‑oriented (Premise 2), we can directly infer Lia is goal‑oriented. The rule applies universally, so the specific instance of Lia satisfies its antecedent, yielding the consequent.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Lia being strategic and the rule that strategic implies goal‑oriented, we infer Lia is goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(lia)</logic>
</step>

Having established Lia as goal‑oriented in the previous step, we now apply the rule that goal‑oriented individuals are talented (Premise 3). This modus ponens step gives us Lia's talent, which will be needed to resolve the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since Lia is goal‑oriented and goal‑oriented implies talented, we deduce Lia is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(lia)</logic>
</step>

We now possess Lia's talent, making the left side of the disjunction (~talented(lia) v ambitious(lia)) false. Because the disjunction must be true, the only way to satisfy it is for the right side—Lia being ambitious—to hold. Thus, we conclude Lia is ambitious.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given Lia is talented and the disjunction that either not talented or ambitious holds, we resolve that Lia must be ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(lia)</logic>
</step>

With Lia's ambition established, we return to the first rule (Premise 1) that ambition guarantees competitiveness. Applying this rule to Lia yields the desired conclusion that Lia is competitive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because Lia is ambitious and ambition leads to competitiveness, we conclude Lia is competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(lia)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Lia is competitive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the bustling workshop, Sam the Sawyer and Wade the Finisher are known for their upbeat attitudes, while all three carpenters—Sam, Victor the Joiner, and Wade—take a hands‑on approach to every project. Their practical style means they are always ready to tackle a task, which in turn leaves them feeling well restored after a day's work. The workshop has a simple set of expectations: anyone who feels well restored tends to be optimistic, and an optimistic carpenter is considered wise by the master craftsman.

examples.context: In the robotics lab, two service units, Alpha Bot and Beta Bot, perform routine checks on the assembly line. Alpha Bot is known to be optimistic, while Beta Bot shares that optimism and is also praised for being especially resourceful. The engineers have recorded several conditional behaviors: whenever a unit is resourceful it can operate at high speed, an optimistic unit tends to be smart, a functional unit is automatically considered resourceful, a unit that is not robust is declared ready, and a robust unit cannot be high‑speed. Additionally, the system logs indicate that either a unit is not smart or it is functional. The team now wonders whether Alpha Bot fails to be ready.
examples.question: Is Alpha Bot not ready?
examples.completion:
<think>
The problem asks us to decide whether Alpha Bot fails to be ready. To answer, we must trace the chain of conditions that connect Alpha Bot's known optimism to its readiness status.

This premise tells us that resourcefulness is sufficient for high‑speed operation. It will become useful later when we manage to prove Alpha Bot is resourceful, allowing us to derive a speed property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Alpha Bot is resourceful, it can move at high speed.</text>
<logic>high_speed(alpha_bot) :- resourceful(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a direct link from optimism to being smart. Since Alpha Bot is optimistic, this rule opens a path toward establishing other attributes that depend on smartness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Alpha Bot is optimistic, then it is smart.</text>
<logic>smart(alpha_bot) :- optimistic(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The third rule converts functional status into resourcefulness. Once we know Alpha Bot is functional, we can immediately claim it is resourceful, which then feeds into other rules.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being functional guarantees that Alpha Bot is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(alpha_bot) :- functional(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

This conditional explains how the absence of robustness leads to readiness. It will be crucial after we manage to show Alpha Bot is not robust, giving us the desired ready status.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Alpha Bot becomes ready whenever it is not robust.</text>
<logic>ready(alpha_bot) :- ~robust(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise creates a negative relation: a robust unit cannot be high‑speed. It will allow us to argue that if Alpha Bot is high‑speed, it cannot be robust, thereby supporting the antecedent of the readiness rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Alpha Bot is robust, then it cannot be high‑speed.</text>
<logic>~high_speed(alpha_bot) :- robust(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides a logical alternative: either Alpha Bot is not smart or it is functional. This will let us combine a known smart fact with the disjunction to force the functional conclusion.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The system states that either Alpha Bot is not smart or it is functional.</text>
<logic>~smart(alpha_bot) v functional(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

We are given explicitly that Alpha Bot is optimistic. This fact is the starting point for applying the optimism‑to‑smart rule and launching the inference chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Alpha Bot is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

By applying the optimism‑to‑smart rule to the fact that Alpha Bot is optimistic, we deduce that Alpha Bot is smart. This step transforms a basic mood attribute into a cognitive one, setting up the use of the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Alpha Bot's optimism we infer that Alpha Bot is smart.</text>
<logic>smart(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says Alpha Bot is either not smart or functional. Since we have just established that Alpha Bot is smart, the 'not smart' alternative is false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Alpha Bot must be functional. This classic disjunctive syllogism gives us functional status.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Using the disjunction, the fact that Alpha Bot is smart forces Alpha Bot to be functional.</text>
<logic>functional(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Alpha Bot is functional, we invoke the functional‑to‑resourceful rule. This yields that Alpha Bot is resourceful, which will later enable us to claim high‑speed operation via the first premise.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since Alpha Bot is functional, we can conclude that Alpha Bot is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

Now that Alpha Bot is resourceful, the resourceful‑to‑high‑speed rule applies, allowing us to infer that Alpha Bot is high‑speed. This new speed attribute will be pivotal for establishing non‑robustness in the subsequent step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>With Alpha Bot being resourceful, we deduce that Alpha Bot is high‑speed.</text>
<logic>high_speed(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

We know Alpha Bot is high‑speed, and the robust‑to‑not‑high‑speed rule tells us a robust unit cannot be high‑speed. Since Alpha Bot does have the high‑speed property, it cannot be robust; therefore, we conclude Alpha Bot is not robust. This negative robustness is exactly what we need for the readiness rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Because Alpha Bot is high‑speed and a robust unit cannot be high‑speed, we infer that Alpha Bot is not robust.</text>
<logic>~robust(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

With the established fact that Alpha Bot is not robust, we can finally apply the not‑robust‑to‑ready rule. This gives us the conclusion that Alpha Bot is ready, directly addressing the query about its readiness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>From Alpha Bot not being robust we obtain that Alpha Bot is ready.</text>
<logic>ready(alpha_bot)</logic>
</step>

We derived Alpha_bot is ready, so the conclusion Alpha_bot is not ready is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Given the relationships among strategic, brave, inquisitive, inventive, disciplined, gifted, and technique_focused, can we conclude that fighter_mark is technique_focused?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether fighter_mark possesses the property technique_focused. To answer this, we must trace how the given facts and rules about strategic, brave, inquisitive, inventive, disciplined, and gifted attributes interact for fighter_mark.

This premise tells us that strategic ability always brings bravery. It will let us turn fighter_mark's strategic status into a brave one later in the proof.

Here we learn that inquisitiveness is a source of inventiveness. This connection will be useful once we establish that fighter_mark is inquisitive.

The rule states that lacking giftedness guarantees technique focus. This is the crucial bridge to our final goal, but we first need to show fighter_mark is not gifted.

Inventiveness leads to being disciplined, linking creative skill to a more ordered mindset. This will help us derive a contradiction if fighter_mark were gifted.

</step>

If a practitioner is gifted, they lose discipline. By combining this with the previous rule, we can use disciplined status to argue that fighter_mark cannot be gifted.
<step n="5" type="premise">

<logic>~disciplined(fighter_mark) :- gifted(fighter_mark)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either fighter_mark is not brave or he is inquisitive. Since we will soon prove he is brave, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for him to be inquisitive.

<text>Either fighter_mark is not brave or fighter_mark is inquisitive.</text>
<logic>~brave(fighter_mark) v inquisitive(fighter_mark)</logic>

A direct fact gives us that fighter_mark is strategic. This is the starting point for the chain of deductions that follow.

<logic>strategic(fighter_mark)</logic>
</step>

Applying the strategic‑to‑brave rule to the fact that fighter_mark is strategic yields bravery. This step converts a known attribute into another that participates in the disjunction later.

<text>From fighter_mark being strategic and the rule that strategic implies brave, we infer that fighter_mark is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(fighter_mark)</logic>
</step>

We now have fighter_mark brave, but the disjunction says "not brave or inquisitive" must be true. Since "not brave" is false, the only way for the statement to hold is for fighter_mark to be inquisitive, which we therefore infer.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">

<logic>inquisitive(fighter_mark)</logic>

With inquisitiveness established, we invoke the rule that inquisitive practitioners become inventive. This gives us the inventive property for fighter_mark, moving us toward disciplined status.

examples.context: In a competitive market, two firms—Firm Alpha and Firm Beta—are evaluating their strategic positions. Both firms are known for launching new products quickly, which makes them innovative. Analysts have observed that an innovative firm tends to operate efficiently, and that a financially robust firm is usually willing to take on risk. Moreover, for Firm Beta there is a logical observation: either it is not efficient or it possesses strong financial health. The question is whether Firm Beta can be considered risk tolerant based on these observations.
examples.question: Is Firm Beta risk tolerant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Firm Beta can be classified as risk tolerant. To answer this, we must trace how the given facts and logical rules allow us to derive risk tolerance for Firm Beta.

This rule tells us that financial robustness is sufficient for a firm to be risk tolerant. It will be essential later because once we establish that Firm Beta is financially robust, we can immediately claim it is risk tolerant.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is financially robust then it is risk tolerant.</text>
<logic>risk_tolerant(firm_beta) :- financially_robust(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that innovation guarantees efficiency. Since we already know Firm Beta is innovative, this link will let us infer its efficiency, which is a stepping stone toward financial robustness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a firm is innovative then it is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(firm_beta) :- innovative(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers a logical bridge: either a firm lacks efficiency or it enjoys financial robustness. When we later confirm that Firm Beta is efficient, the first alternative becomes false, forcing the second alternative—financial robustness—to be true.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the firm is not efficient or it is financially robust.</text>
<logic>~efficient(firm_beta) v financially_robust(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Firm Beta is innovative gives us a concrete starting point. It activates the rule from Premise 2, allowing us to move forward in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Firm Beta is innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

By applying the rule that innovation implies efficiency to the fact that Firm Beta is innovative, we conclude that Firm Beta is efficient. This step is straightforward: the premise directly supplies the condition needed for the rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Firm Beta's innovation and the rule linking innovation to efficiency, we infer that Firm Beta is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Firm Beta's efficiency, we examine the disjunction from Premise 3. Because the 'not efficient' option is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the 'financially robust' option to be true, so we infer that Firm Beta is financially robust.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that Firm Beta is efficient and the disjunction that either not efficient or financially robust holds, we conclude that Firm Beta must be financially robust.</text>
<logic>financially_robust(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

With Firm Beta confirmed as financially robust, we can invoke the rule from Premise 1, which states that financial robustness leads to risk tolerance. Consequently, we deduce that Firm Beta is risk tolerant.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since Firm Beta is financially robust and the rule that financial robustness leads to risk tolerance, we deduce that Firm Beta is risk tolerant.</text>
<logic>risk_tolerant(firm_beta)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Firm_beta is risk tolerant, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the laboratory, chemists often compare the behavior of simple carbonyl compounds like acetone with aromatic hydrocarbons such as benzene. Acetone is notorious for its high reactivity, readily participating in nucleophilic attacks, while benzene is celebrated for its aromatic stability. Certain chemical traits cascade: a reactive molecule tends to exhibit polarity, a polar molecule can display aromatic character under the right resonance conditions, and an aromatic molecule is frequently regarded as chemically stable. By tracing these connections, we can infer deeper properties of the substances involved.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that acetone is stable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether acetone can be regarded as stable. The goal is to chain together the provided chemical rules so that the stability of acetone follows logically from its known reactivity.

The first rule links aromaticity to stability, stating that any compound that is aromatic is also stable. This connection will be crucial later when we manage to show that acetone is aromatic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If acetone were aromatic, then it would be classified as stable.</text>
<logic>stable(acetone) :- aromatic(acetone)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that polarity implies aromaticity. By establishing that acetone is polar, we will be able to move one step closer to proving its aromatic nature.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If acetone were polar, then it would be considered aromatic.</text>
<logic>aromatic(acetone) :- polar(acetone)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides the bridge from reactivity to polarity, asserting that a reactive compound must be polar. This allows us to start the chain from a fact we already know about acetone.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If acetone were reactive, then it would possess polarity.</text>
<logic>polar(acetone) :- reactive(acetone)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that acetone is reactive, which is a factual observation from experimental chemistry. This fact serves as the foundation for the entire inference sequence.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Acetone is known to be reactive.</text>
<logic>reactive(acetone)</logic>
</step>

Using the rule that reactive compounds are polar, we combine the known reactivity of acetone with that rule to derive that acetone is polar. The inference is straightforward: the premise matches the antecedent of the rule, so the consequent follows.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because acetone is reactive, we can infer that it is polar.</text>
<logic>polar(acetone)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established acetone's polarity, we apply the second rule which says polarity leads to aromaticity. Because the condition of the rule is satisfied, we can confidently assert that acetone is aromatic.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Given that acetone is polar, we can deduce that it is aromatic.</text>
<logic>aromatic(acetone)</logic>
</step>

With acetone identified as aromatic, we invoke the first rule linking aromaticity to stability. The antecedent of the rule holds, allowing us to conclude that acetone is indeed stable.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since acetone is aromatic, we conclude that it is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(acetone)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Acetone is stable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the corporate data center, router_felix is a critical piece of infrastructure. It has been marked as secure because it handles sensitive traffic, and administrators have classified it as high_priority to guarantee rapid response to any issues. The network management system follows a set of default policies: a router that has monitoring_enabled becomes online unless an abnormal active block is present; monitoring_enabled is turned on for any high_priority router unless a monitoring anomaly is reported; and a secure router is considered not online unless a special active exception overrides the rule. These policies interact to determine the operational state of each device.
examples.question: Given the facts and default policies, is it true that router_felix is not scalable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that router_felix is not scalable holds. To answer this, we must trace how the given facts and default policies determine the router's scalability status.

This premise tells us that scalability is a direct consequence of being online. It is crucial because if we can establish that router_felix is online, we will immediately obtain its scalability, which directly impacts the query.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a router is online, then it is scalable.</text>
<logic>scalable(router_felix) :- online(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

Here we see the condition that makes a router online: having monitoring_enabled and lacking an abnormal active block. This connects the monitoring state to the online state, providing a bridge we will need later.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>A router becomes online whenever it has monitoring_enabled and there is no abnormal active blockage.</text>
<logic>online(router_felix) :- monitoring_enabled(router_felix), not ab_active_a(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

This rule creates monitoring_enabled from the high_priority attribute, assuming no monitoring anomaly. It gives us a way to derive the monitoring condition from the known high_priority fact about router_felix.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Monitoring_enabled is granted to any router that is high_priority, provided there is no abnormal monitoring issue.</text>
<logic>monitoring_enabled(router_felix) :- high_priority(router_felix), not ab_monitoring_enabled(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

This default rule introduces a potential obstacle: a secure router is normally considered not online. It will compete with the rule that makes a router online, so we must consider which rule takes priority later in the derivation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>A router is not online if it is secure, unless an abnormal active exception applies.</text>
<logic>~online(router_felix) :- secure(router_felix), not ab_active_b(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

The fact that router_felix is high_priority is the only explicit fact we start with besides its security classification. It will trigger the rule that yields monitoring_enabled.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Router_felix is high_priority.</text>
<logic>high_priority(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

Using the high_priority fact together with the rule that high_priority implies monitoring_enabled, and noting that no abnormal monitoring issue is mentioned, we can safely infer that router_felix now has monitoring_enabled. This step provides the antecedent needed for the online rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From router_felix being high_priority and the rule that high_priority routers get monitoring_enabled, we infer that router_felix has monitoring_enabled.</text>
<logic>monitoring_enabled(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

At this point we have monitoring_enabled for router_felix, so the rule that monitoring_enabled leads to being online becomes applicable. Although there is a competing rule that secure routers are not online, the system's priority mechanism prefers the positive derivation when no abnormal active exception blocks it. Consequently, we conclude that router_felix is online.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2,4">
<text>Because router_felix has monitoring_enabled, the rule that monitoring_enabled leads to being online applies; the competing rule that secure routers are not online is overridden by priority, so we conclude router_felix is online.</text>
<logic>online(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

Having established that router_felix is online, we can now apply the first premise, which states that any online router is scalable. This straightforward inference gives us the scalability status of router_felix.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since router_felix is online, the rule linking online status to scalability lets us conclude that router_felix is scalable.</text>
<logic>scalable(router_felix)</logic>
</step>

We derived Router_felix is scalable, so the conclusion Router_felix is not scalable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Patient Alex enrolled in a new clinical trial and immediately showed a cooperative attitude toward the study staff. Because of this openness, the investigators were able to assess his logical reasoning about the protocol without any objections. The trial designers noted that a cooperative participant often becomes engaged with the treatment plan, and an engaged participant is typically deemed eligible for the next phase of the study. The overall goal is to determine whether Alex will adhere strictly to the medication schedule prescribed by the researchers.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Alex adherent to the treatment protocol?
examples.completion:
<think>
The problem asks whether the trial participant, Alex, can be considered adherent to the medication schedule. To answer this we must trace how the given facts and default rules connect cooperation, logical reasoning, engagement, and eligibility to adherence.

This rule tells us that adherence follows from eligibility as long as nothing explicitly blocks it. It sets up the final link we need: once we can show Alex is eligible, the lack of an 'ab_adherent' exception will let us assert his adherence.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is eligible and there is no evidence contradicting his adherence, then we can infer that Alex is adherent.</text>
<logic>adherent(patient_alex) :- eligible(patient_alex), not ab_adherent(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

Here the rule connects logical thinking to engagement, again assuming no contrary evidence. It is important because engagement is the next step toward establishing eligibility in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Alex is logical and there is no evidence against his engagement, we may conclude that Alex is engaged.</text>
<logic>engaged(patient_alex) :- logical(patient_alex), not ab_engaged(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

This default says that engagement yields eligibility, provided there is no evidence refuting eligibility. It creates the bridge from the earlier derived engagement to the eligibility condition required by PREMISE_1.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is engaged and no contrary evidence exists about his eligibility, then Alex is eligible.</text>
<logic>eligible(patient_alex) :- engaged(patient_alex), not ab_eligible(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

The rule links Alex's cooperative demeanor to logical reasoning, again under the assumption that no counter‑evidence exists. It supplies the initial logical fact needed to start the cascade of defaults.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Being cooperative leads to Alex being logical, provided there is no evidence opposing his logicality.</text>
<logic>logical(patient_alex) :- cooperative(patient_alex), not ab_logical(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

The only concrete fact we have is Alex's cooperative nature. This serves as the anchor for the whole inference process, allowing us to activate the rule in PREMISE_4.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We know that Alex is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Alex is cooperative and the rule in PREMISE_4, we infer that Alex is logical. Then, applying PREMISE_2, the logical status gives us that Alex is engaged, because there is no 'ab_engaged' information to block it. Thus STEP_6 produces the engaged predicate.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4,2">
<text>From Alex being cooperative we obtain that he is logical, and from this logical status we derive that Alex is engaged.</text>
<logic>engaged(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

With Alex now known to be engaged, PREMISE_3 lets us derive that Alex is eligible, assuming again no contrary evidence. Finally, PREMISE_1 uses this eligibility to conclude that Alex is adherent, since the 'ab_adherent' exception is absent. This chain yields the desired adherence conclusion.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3,1">
<text>Since Alex is engaged, we infer that he is eligible, and with no evidence against his adherence we finally conclude that Alex is adherent.</text>
<logic>adherent(patient_alex)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Patient_alex is adherent, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: A wind engineer working on offshore turbines is known for deep technical knowledge. Finally, sustainable‑oriented engineers are typically eligible for funding, provided no overriding objection is recorded.
examples.question: Is it the case that the wind engineer is not funded?

We are asked to determine whether the wind engineer fails to receive funding. To answer, we must trace how the given facts and rules propagate properties such as certification, innovativeness, and sustainability, ultimately affecting funding status. The goal is to see if the system can derive a funded status, which would make the negated query false.

This premise tells us that certification automatically grants the label of innovative. It is crucial because later steps will need the innovative attribute to trigger sustainability considerations, which in turn influence funding.

<text>If the wind engineer is certified, then they are considered innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(wind_engineer) :- certified(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

Here, expertise leads to certification by the board. Since we already have a fact that the engineer is an expert, this rule forms the bridge from a concrete skill to the abstract status of certification, setting up the chain toward innovativeness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the wind engineer is an expert, the certification board will certify them.</text>
<logic>certified(wind_engineer) :- expert(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

The funding default rule says that an engineer receives funding when they are sustainable‑oriented and there is no specific objection. This rule will be applied once we can show the engineer is sustainable‑oriented, linking earlier derived properties to the final funding conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>An engineer is funded when they are sustainable‑oriented and there is no explicit objection to funding.</text>
<logic>funded(wind_engineer) :- sustainable_oriented(wind_engineer), not ab_funded(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

This rule makes sustainable orientation depend on innovativeness, again assuming no objection. It connects the innovative property (derived from certification) to the sustainability attribute, which is a prerequisite for funding according to the previous rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>An engineer becomes sustainable‑oriented when they are innovative and there is no explicit objection to sustainability orientation.</text>
<logic>sustainable_oriented(wind_engineer) :- innovative(wind_engineer), not ab_sustainable_oriented(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the wind engineer is an expert provides the starting point for the chain of reasoning. It directly satisfies the antecedent of the certification rule, allowing us to move forward toward certification and beyond.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The wind engineer is an expert in turbine design.</text>
<logic>expert(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

By combining the expert fact with the rule that expertise yields certification, we can infer that the engineer is certified. This step is straightforward: the antecedent of the certification rule matches the known fact, so the consequent follows, giving us a new piece of information to work with.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the fact that the engineer is an expert and the rule linking expertise to certification, we conclude the engineer is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

With certification now established, we apply the rule that certification implies innovativeness. Since the engineer satisfies the antecedent (being certified), we can deduce that they are innovative. This property is essential for triggering the sustainability rule in the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Using the certification and the rule that certification implies innovativeness, we infer the engineer is innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

Because the engineer is innovative, the sustainability rule activates, granting them the sustainable‑oriented label, assuming no objection. With sustainable orientation in hand, the funding default rule's antecedents are both satisfied, leading us to conclude that the engineer is funded. The chain thus moves from expertise to certification, to innovativeness, to sustainability, and finally to funding.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4,3">
<text>Because the engineer is innovative, the sustainability rule yields that they are sustainable‑oriented; together with the funding rule, we derive that the engineer is funded.</text>
<logic>funded(wind_engineer)</logic>
</step>

We derived Wind_engineer is funded, so the conclusion Wind_engineer is not funded is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Field biologists have been observing two charismatic mammals in the tundra: an arctic fox and a snowy owl. The fox has earned a reputation for being recognized by the research team for its clever hunting tactics, while the owl is noted for its unbiased approach to hunting in varying weather. Researchers also keep track of behavioral traits such as nurturing, assertiveness, low‑profile conduct, timeliness, and robustness. A set of ecological rules connects these traits: if an animal is assertive, it tends to be nurturing; timeliness follows from being recognized; low‑profile behavior follows from timeliness; robustness follows from being unbiased; and a disjunction states that either the owl is not robust or it is assertive. Additionally, a default principle says that any animal that is low‑profile will be social unless there is explicit evidence to the contrary. The scientists now wonder whether the arctic fox can be classified as social.
examples.question: Based on the given facts and rules, is the arctic fox social?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the arctic fox can be classified as social. To answer this, we must trace how the provided facts and rules allow us to derive the social predicate for the fox.

This rule connects assertiveness with nurturing, which may become relevant later if we ever establish assertiveness for an animal. Although it does not directly involve the fox now, it sets up a possible chain of implications.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever an animal is assertive, it is also nurturing.</text>
<logic>low_profile(arctic_fox) :- timely(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that timeliness implies a low‑profile demeanor. This link will be crucial because we will soon obtain timeliness for the fox, enabling us to infer low‑profile status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If an animal is timely, then it is low‑profile.</text>
<logic>timely(arctic_fox) :- recognized(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that any recognized animal is timely. Since we have a fact that the fox is recognized, this premise will let us derive timeliness for the fox.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being recognized guarantees that the animal is timely.</text>
<logic>social(arctic_fox) :- low_profile(arctic_fox), not ab_social(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

This connects being unbiased with robustness. It will be useful for reasoning about the owl, but it does not affect the fox directly.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>An unbiased animal is considered robust.</text>
<logic>recognized(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Arctic_fox is timely.</text>
<logic>timely(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Arctic_fox is low profile.</text>
<logic>low_profile(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>By default reasoning (no known exception), we derive Arctic_fox is social.</text>
<logic>social(arctic_fox)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Arctic_fox is social, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The default rule for assessment says that an incumbent_exempt candidate is automatically vetted, unless something abnormal prevents the assessment. This rule will let us move from exemption to the vetted status required for enthusiasm.

The second default rule provides a way to obtain the exemption: a strategic candidate becomes incumbent_exempt, again assuming no abnormality blocks it. This creates the bridge from strategy to exemption, and then to vetting.

We are given directly that Charlie is eligible, which is the factual anchor for the whole derivation. From this fact we can start applying the rule that turns eligibility into policy focus.

Using the eligibility‑to‑policy_focus rule, we take the fact that Charlie is eligible and conclude that he is policy_focused. This step is a straightforward application of modus ponens, moving us one link forward in the chain.

Now that we know Charlie is policy_focused, we can invoke the rule that policy_focused implies strategic. By applying modus ponens again, we obtain that Charlie is strategic, setting up the conditions for the exemption rule.

Because we know Rowan is open to evidence and the rule states that openness implies systematic behavior, we can directly infer that Rowan is systematic. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, turning the antecedent into a new fact.

examples.context: In the community plot, the garden warden tends to the beds with great care. This rule tells us how pruning translates into the garden being recorded as seeded. It will be useful later because once we have pruning we can immediately obtain the seeded status needed for the next steps.

<logic>seeded(garden_warden) :- pruned(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that a soil‑rich condition forces the warden to prune. This connects the initial fact about the soil to the pruning predicate, allowing us to move forward in the reasoning chain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being soil‑rich causes the garden warden to prune the garden.</text>
<logic>pruned(garden_warden) :- soil_rich(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

The default rule captures the normal expectation that a seeded garden is watered unless something abnormal blocks it. It introduces the watered predicate, which is a prerequisite for reaching sunshine.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Unless there is an abnormal reason not to, a seeded garden leads to the garden being watered.</text>
<logic>watered(garden_warden) :- seeded(garden_warden), not ab_watered(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

This rule states that a watered garden, in the absence of abnormal interference, will be sunny. It is the final link that will let us derive the sunny property we need to evaluate the query.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the garden is watered and there is no abnormal reason, then the garden becomes sunny.</text>
<logic>sunny(garden_warden) :- watered(garden_warden), not ab_sunny(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

The only concrete fact we start with is that the garden warden's soil is rich. This fact activates the rule about pruning and sets the whole chain in motion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The garden warden's soil is rich.</text>
<logic>soil_rich(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the soil is rich together with the rule that richness leads to pruning, we can infer that the warden indeed prunes the garden. This step bridges the initial observation to the first predicate in the chain.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the rich soil we infer that the garden warden prunes the garden.</text>
<logic>pruned(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established pruning, we apply the rule linking pruning to seeding. Consequently, the garden is recognized as seeded, which is required for the default watering rule to apply.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because the warden prunes, we can conclude that the garden is seeded.</text>
<logic>seeded(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

With the garden seeded, the default rule tells us it will be watered unless there is an abnormal reason, which we have no evidence for. Then, applying the sunshine rule, a watered garden without abnormal hindrance becomes sunny, giving us the desired sunny property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3,4">
<text>With the garden seeded and no abnormal obstacles, the garden is watered and therefore becomes sunny.</text>
<logic>sunny(garden_warden)</logic>
</step>

We derived Garden_warden is sunny, so the conclusion Garden_warden is not sunny is False.
</think>

The second premise links the nebula's stability to it being observed, which is a prerequisite for the default rule about high‑resolution. Recognizing this bridge is essential for moving from a stability fact to an observation fact.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever nebula_alpha is stable, observers consider it to have been properly observed.</text>
<logic>observed(nebula_alpha) :- stable(nebula_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The default rule provides a way to infer high‑resolution from observation, as long as there is no conflicting archival record for the same object. This rule will become active once we have shown that nebula_alpha is observed and that no archival high‑resolution entry exists for it.

The factual statement that nebula_alpha is stable gives us a concrete starting point. Stability is the trigger for the observation rule introduced in premise two.

Now that we know nebula_alpha is observed, we check the conditions of the default rule. There is no archival high‑resolution entry for nebula_alpha itself—only galaxy_beta has such an entry—so the 'not archival_high_res' condition is satisfied. Consequently, the default rule allows us to assert that nebula_alpha is high‑resolution.

An apparent obstacle might be the existence of an archival high‑resolution image for galaxy_beta, but the default rule's exception only concerns the same entity. <step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Even though galaxy_beta possesses an archival high‑resolution image, this does not prevent the default from applying to nebula_alpha, so nebula_alpha remains high‑resolution.</text>

Therefore Nebula_alpha is cataloged, so the conclusion is True.

Answer: True

The third rule links humility to empathy for Charlie. Because we have a direct fact that Charlie is humble, this rule will let us move from humility to empathy, an essential step toward establishing a rating.

examples.context: Alex is a psychology graduate student who consistently shows a keen insight into human behavior. Because Alex is insightful, his peers often seek his perspective on complex cases. He is currently enrolled in the advanced research program, which automatically places him on the department's evaluation list. The program's policies state that anyone who is evaluated and shows no procedural irregularities is considered studious. Moreover, the department believes that a studious mindset fosters resilience, provided no exceptional barriers exist. These interlocking expectations shape Alex's academic profile.
examples.question: Is Alex driven?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Alex exhibits a driven attitude according to the psychological framework provided. The goal is to trace how the various attributes and default rules interact to support or defeat the claim that Alex is driven.

The first premise connects resilience to driven behavior, establishing a direct conditional link that will be useful later. Recognizing this relationship helps us see that proving resilience will be sufficient for our final goal.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is resilient, then Alex is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(alex) :- resilient(alex)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that enrollment automatically triggers an evaluation, creating a bridge from a concrete fact to a more abstract status. This rule is essential because it lets us move from a known fact about Alex to a predicate that participates in later defaults.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is enrolled, then Alex is evaluated.</text>
<logic>evaluated(alex) :- enrolled(alex)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a default rule: if someone is evaluated and no abnormality blocks scholarly status, they become studious. This captures a typical departmental expectation and will allow us to derive a studious attribute once we have evaluation.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is evaluated and there is no abnormality preventing scholarly status, then Alex is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(alex) :- evaluated(alex), not ab_studious(alex)</logic>
</step>

This default states that a studious individual becomes resilient unless an abnormality intervenes. It provides the crucial step from the academic quality of being studious to the personal quality of resilience, which we need for the final inference.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is studious and there is no abnormality preventing resilience, then Alex is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(alex) :- studious(alex), not ab_strong_a(alex)</logic>
</step>

Conversely, this default asserts that an insightful person is taken to be not resilient unless a blocking abnormality is present. It introduces a potential conflict with the previous default, setting the stage for a priority decision later on.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is insightful and there is no abnormality preventing the negation of resilience, then Alex is not resilient.</text>
<logic>~resilient(alex) :- insightful(alex), not ab_strong_b(alex)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Alex is enrolled is a concrete observation about his current status in the program. This fact will activate the enrollment‑to‑evaluation rule, starting the chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Alex is enrolled.</text>
<logic>enrolled(alex)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact that Alex is enrolled and the rule that enrollment implies evaluation yields the conclusion that Alex is evaluated. This step converts a concrete enrollment fact into the evaluative status required by the default rule about scholarly behavior.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Alex being enrolled and the rule that enrollment implies evaluation, we infer that Alex is evaluated.</text>
<logic>evaluated(alex)</logic>
</step>

With Alex now known to be evaluated, the default rule about scholarly status can fire, assuming no abnormality blocks it. Since we have no information about such an abnormality, we safely infer that Alex is studious, moving us closer to resilience.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because Alex is evaluated and the default rule that evaluation leads to being studious (unless blocked), we conclude Alex is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(alex)</logic>
</step>

At this point we have two competing defaults: one that would make Alex not resilient because he is insightful, and another that would make him resilient because he is studious. The system's priority mechanism favors the rule deriving resilience from studiousness over the contradictory default, so we adopt the resilient conclusion. This resolution is critical because it provides the antecedent needed for the final driven inference.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4,5">
<text>Given Alex is studious, the rule that studiousness yields resilience (unless blocked) competes with the default that insightfulness yields non‑resilience; the priority mechanism selects resilience, so Alex is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(alex)</logic>
</step>

Now that Alex is established as resilient, we can invoke the first premise linking resilience to driven behavior. By modus ponens, this yields the desired conclusion that Alex is driven, directly answering the query.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Alex is resilient and the rule linking resilience to being driven, we derive that Alex is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(alex)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Alex is driven, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: On the craggy faces of the Alpine range, two climbers, Ross and Zane, are preparing for a challenging ascent. Ross is noted for his nimble movements and his knack for finding clever solutions to unexpected obstacles, while Zane is praised for his steady composure on the rope. In this climbing community, it is customary to assume that any climber who has proven themselves experienced will act steady unless someone has explicitly reported them as unsteady. Likewise, a climber who shows resourceful problem‑solving is taken to be tactical unless there is clear evidence that they lack tactical judgment. These default assumptions help the team coordinate safety decisions when concrete observations are missing.

The default rule fills gaps in our knowledge: if we can show Ross is experienced, we may assume he is steady unless contradicted. This assumption is essential because we have no explicit observation of Ross's steadiness, only a rule that can generate it from experience.

This default rule provides a way to infer tactical ability from resourcefulness, again in the absence of contrary evidence. Since we know Ross is resourceful, the rule gives us a path to claim he is tactical, which will later let us infer experience.

The fact that Ross is resourceful gives us a concrete starting point for the chain of reasoning. It activates the default rule about tactical ability, allowing us to move forward without any additional observations.

</step>

Combining Ross's resourcefulness with the default tactical rule, we see there is no evidence that Ross lacks tactical skill, so we can safely infer he is tactical. This inference is the first derived fact that moves us from a simple observation to a more complex attribute.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">

With Ross confirmed as experienced, the default rule about steadiness kicks in because we have no indication that Ross is unsteady. Consequently, we can conclude Ross is steady, satisfying the premise that links steadiness to thoroughness.

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">

Now that we know Frankie's discerning, we can apply the second rule, which states that discerning leads to quick-wittedness. Consequently, we conclude that Frankie's quick-witted, providing the crucial attribute for the fairness default.

examples.context: On the merchant schooner Aurora, the first mate is known for his deep knowledge of navigation, while the deckhand follows the ship's daily routine without fail. The crew values skill at the helm, vigilance on watch, and a clear chain of responsibility. When a crew member performs their duties routinely, they become watchful, and a watchful crew member earns a senior rank on board. Likewise, a skilled sailor who is also a quick learner is praised for being steady at the helm.
examples.question: Is the deckhand senior?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the deckhand holds a senior rank on the ship. The goal is to trace the logical chain from the known facts about the deckhand's routine duties to the senior status.

This rule links the property of being skilled to being steady at the helm for the first mate. It will be useful later if we ever need to establish the first mate's steadiness, though it is not directly needed for the deckhand's rank.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the first mate is skilled, then they are steady at the helm.</text>
<logic>watchful(deckhand) :- routine(deckhand)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a conditional that turns a routine habit into watchfulness for the deckhand. Since we already know the deckhand is routine, this premise forms a crucial bridge toward seniority.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the deckhand follows a routine, then they are watchful.</text>
<logic>senior(deckhand) :- watchful(deckhand)</logic>
</step>

This statement tells us that watchfulness automatically grants senior rank. Once we can show the deckhand is watchful, we can immediately infer seniority, which is exactly what we need for the query.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the deckhand is watchful, then they are senior.</text>
<logic>routine(deckhand)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Deckhand is watchful.</text>
<logic>watchful(deckhand)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Deckhand is senior.</text>
<logic>senior(deckhand)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Deckhand is senior, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Kate is a rising star in competitive sprinting, admired for her natural talent on the track. Her coaches often note that her raw ability translates into a fierce drive to improve every season. When a runner shows such determination, the team usually treats her as a true team player, assuming nothing abnormal interferes with that perception. Moreover, athletes who are embraced by their teammates tend to receive official certification from the national federation, again provided no irregularities block the process. All of these observations together create a web of expectations about Kate's athletic profile.
examples.question: Given the facts and rules, can we conclude that Kate is hardworking?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Kate can be classified as hardworking. The goal is to trace a chain of reasons from the given facts and default rules to the target property.

This rule tells us that certification is sufficient for hardworking status. It will be useful later once we can establish that Kate is certified.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Kate is certified, then she is considered hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(kate) :- certified(kate)</logic>
</step>

Here we see a direct link between talent and being driven. Since we already have a fact about Kate's talent, this rule will allow us to derive her drivenness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Kate is talented, then she is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(kate) :- talented(kate)</logic>
</step>

The default rule captures the idea that a driven athlete becomes a team player unless an abnormality prevents it. It sets up a conditional step that will be needed to move from driven to certified.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Kate is driven and there is no abnormality preventing her from being a team player, then she is a team player.</text>
<logic>team_player(kate) :- driven(kate), not ab_team_player(kate)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, this default rule says a team player becomes certified in the absence of any abnormality. It bridges the gap between being a team player and obtaining certification, which is crucial for the final goal.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Kate is a team player and no abnormality blocks certification, then she is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(kate) :- team_player(kate), not ab_certified(kate)</logic>
</step>

The fact directly states that Kate possesses talent. This is the starting point for the chain of inferences that will lead to her being hardworking.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Kate is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(kate)</logic>
</step>

Combining the talent fact with the rule that talent implies drivenness, we can safely assert that Kate is driven. This step converts the raw fact into a property that feeds the default rules.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since Kate is talented, we infer that she is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(kate)</logic>
</step>

With Kate now known to be driven, we apply the default rule that a driven athlete becomes a team player, assuming no abnormality. Once we have her as a team player, the second default rule gives us certification, again under the normality assumption. Thus we obtain that Kate is certified.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3,4">
<text>Because Kate is driven, and assuming no abnormalities, we first infer she is a team player, which then lets us infer she is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(kate)</logic>
</step>

Now that certification has been established, we invoke the first premise which says certification guarantees hardworking status. This yields the desired conclusion that Kate is hardworking.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Having established that Kate is certified, we conclude she is hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(kate)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Kate is hardworking, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the forensic laboratory, a piece of evidence labeled "robin" is being examined. The analysts note that the sample is cleared for analysis and that its physical condition is intact. The lab follows a set of procedural guidelines: an intact sample is normally considered identifiable unless something abnormal interferes, an identifiable sample is typically processed unless a different abnormality blocks it, and a sample that is cleared for analysis is assumed not to have been processed unless the higher‑priority rule about identification applies. The question is whether this sample does not contain a fingerprint.
examples.question: Does the evidence sample "robin" fail to contain a fingerprint?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the forensic sample named "robin" lacks a fingerprint. To answer, we must trace the logical rules that connect the sample's known properties to the presence or absence of a fingerprint.

The rule linking processing to fingerprint presence tells us that processing is sufficient for a fingerprint to be found. This will become relevant once we establish whether the sample has been processed.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the sample has been processed, then it contains a fingerprint.</text>
<logic>contains_fingerprint(sample_robin) :- processed(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

The default rule about intact samples being identifiable gives us a way to move from the fact that the sample is intact to the property of identifiability, provided no abnormality interferes. It sets up the first step in the chain toward processing.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Normally, an intact sample is identifiable unless an abnormality prevents identification.</text>
<logic>identifiable(sample_robin) :- intact(sample_robin), not ab_identifiable(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

The next default rule says that an identifiable sample is normally processed unless an abnormality stops it. This connects identifiability to processing, which we need for the fingerprint rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Typically, an identifiable sample gets processed unless an abnormality blocks processing.</text>
<logic>processed(sample_robin) :- identifiable(sample_robin), not ab_prepared_a(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

There is a competing default that a cleared‑for‑analysis sample is presumed not processed. However, this rule is of lower priority than the rule that makes processing follow from identifiability, so it can be overridden when the higher‑priority conditions hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a sample is cleared for analysis, it is assumed not processed unless an abnormality overrides this assumption.</text>
<logic>~processed(sample_robin) :- cleared_for_analysis(sample_robin), not ab_prepared_b(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the sample is intact supplies the concrete starting point for applying the default rule about identifiability.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The sample is intact.</text>
<logic>intact(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

Using the default rule from Premise 2 together with the fact that the sample is intact, we can infer that the sample is identifiable. The absence of any noted abnormality lets the rule fire, giving us the first derived property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From its intactness we infer that the sample is identifiable.</text>
<logic>identifiable(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the sample is identifiable, we apply the default rule from Premise 3 to infer that it has been processed. The rule that a cleared sample is not processed (Premise 4) would suggest the opposite, but because the processing rule has higher priority, it overrides the negation and we conclude the sample is indeed processed.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3,4">
<text>Because the sample is identifiable and no abnormality blocks processing, and the rule that a cleared sample is not processed is overridden, we conclude the sample has been processed.</text>
<logic>processed(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the sample is processed, we invoke the direct rule from Premise 1. This rule tells us that any processed sample must contain a fingerprint, so we derive that the sample contains a fingerprint.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since the sample is processed, we infer it contains a fingerprint.</text>
<logic>contains_fingerprint(sample_robin)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sample_robin is contains fingerprint, so the conclusion Sample_robin is not contains fingerprint is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In a bustling restaurant kitchen, Head Chef Charlie is known for his steady approach to prep work, while Sous Chef Marlowe loves experimenting with new ingredients. The staff often talk about how a chef's personality traits influence the dishes they create. A chef who makes quick decisions can bring balanced flavors to a plate, and a chef who is energized tends to produce consistent results. Generous seasoning, called ample spice, usually follows an experimental mindset, and a well‑stocked pantry is a prerequisite for careful cooking. The kitchen also has default expectations: if a chef's pantry is well stocked and there is no reason to doubt their care, they will be considered careful; likewise, an experimental chef who faces no objections will be deemed generous with seasoning.
examples.question: Given the information above, can we conclude that Sous Chef Marlowe is not careful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Sous Chef Marlowe can be said to lack carefulness. The goal is to see if the knowledge base forces Marlowe to be careful, which would make the negation impossible.

This rule links quick decision‑making to the production of balanced flavors. It will be useful later if we can establish that Charlie makes quick decisions, because that could affect other traits in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a chef makes quick decisions, then they will achieve balanced flavors.</text>
<logic>well_stocked(sous_chef) :- ample_spice(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that steadiness leads to being energized. Since we already have that Charlie is steady, this premise lets us derive his energized state, which will interact with the disjunction in PREMISE_4.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a chef is steady, then they become energized.</text>
<logic>careful(sous_chef) :- well_stocked(sous_chef), not ab_careful(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that adding ample spice guarantees a well‑stocked pantry. This connection will be crucial for Marlowe because once we can show she is generous with seasoning, we will obtain a well‑stocked pantry, a condition needed for carefulness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a chef adds ample spice, then their pantry is well stocked.</text>
<logic>ample_spice(sous_chef) :- experimental(sous_chef), not ab_ample_spice(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either Charlie is not energized, or he makes quick decisions. Combined with the result from PREMISE_2 (that Charlie is energized), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Charlie to make quick decisions, which will later allow us to use PREMISE_1.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the chef is not energized or the chef makes quick decisions.</text>
<logic>experimental(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>By default reasoning (no known exception), we derive Sous_chef is ample spice.</text>
<logic>ample_spice(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Sous_chef is well stocked.</text>
<logic>well_stocked(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>By default reasoning (no known exception), we derive Sous_chef is careful.</text>
<logic>careful(sous_chef)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sous_chef is careful, so the conclusion Sous_chef is not careful is False.
</think>
Answer: False
